text: A New Tale of Cutting the Lamp Ming Dynasty, by Qu You Qu You, courtesy name Zongji, pen name Cunzhai, was from Qiantang (now Hangzhou, Zhejiang). He was a renowned literary figure at the beginning of the Ming dynasty. From a young age, he gained a reputation as a poet, but throughout his life, he remained unrecognized despite his talent. He held minor positions such as teacher, instructor, and longshi (a type of official) during the Hongwu era. During the Yongle era, he suffered misfortune due to his poetry and was exiled for ten years. He is the author of the short story collection *A New Tale of Cutting the Lamp* and other works.

《Jian Deng Xin Hua》 Preface One Having compiled strange and marvelous events from ancient and modern times, I created the *Jian Deng Lu*, totaling forty volumes. Those fond of such tales often share recent events, ranging from not more than a century ago to as recent as a few years. These tales accumulate in my mind, becoming more abundant each day. My habitual fascination makes it impossible to stop, so I took up my pen to record them. The events are all joyful, sorrowful, surprising, and strange. The only regret is that my writing is barren and my vocabulary limited, lacking profound insight to elevate them. Once completed, I felt it delved into the realm of strange tales, verging on the bizarre. I kept it in a chest, not wanting it to be circulated. Many visitors heard about it and wanted to see it, and I couldn't refuse them all. I then explained: "The *Shijing*, *Shujing*, *Yijing*, and *Chunqiu* are all works of the sages, considered the great principles and laws for all ages. However, the *Yijing* speaks of dragons fighting in the wild, the *Shujing* records a rooster crowing from a tripod, the *Guofeng* includes poems about licentiousness, and the *Chunqiu* documents chaotic and rebellious events. These cannot be judged by a single criterion." My compilation, though it may not contribute much to the norms of society and morality, still serves to encourage good and punish evil, to pity the poor and mourn the oppressed, and it aligns with the principle that 'speakers should not be punished, and listeners should be warned.' The visitors found my reasoning sound, so I wrote it at the beginning of the volume.

On the first day of June in the year of Wuwu (戊午) of the 11th year of the Hongwu era, Qü You of Shanyang wrote this in the Great Hidden Hall on Mount Wu. **Second Preface to *New Tales Under the Lamp*** In the past, Chen Hong wrote *The Story of Eternal Regret* and *The Tale of the Old Man from the Eastern City*, and people at the time praised his historical talent and all commended him. When one looks at Niu Zengru's *Records of the Mysterious and Strange* and Liu Fu's *Collection of the Green Lock*, they too narrate strange and extraordinary events; whether the events are true or not need not be discussed, but the structure of the work is also well-crafted. My fellow townsman Qü Zongji wrote *New Tales Under the Lamp*; is it not similar to these works? Zongji's determination is firm and diligent, therefore his learning is extensive; his talent is abundant and quick, therefore his writing is rich and abundant. This collection, though a work of minor officials, encourages good and punishes evil, always serving as a moral lesson, and it cannot be said to be without benefit to the world. Especially the ingenious conception and the exquisite choice of words, they shine brightly, forming a unique style of their own. Reading it makes one joyful and dancing with delight, or sad and shedding tears; there are indeed such cases. Unless one is fond of antiquity, erudite, skilled in writing, and discerning in matters, how could one achieve this? As for the writing of *Record of the Autumn Fragrance Pavilion*, it is like Yuan Zhen's *Tale of Yingying*; I will discuss this with Zongji, I wonder if it is truly so.

In the month of May in the thirtieth year of the Hongwu era, Ling Yunhan of Qiantang wrote the preface to the third preface of *Jian Deng Xin Hua*: I have read Mr. Zongji's *Jian Deng Xin Hua*. Its style is akin to that of legends, and its meaning is like the allegorical stories of the Zhuangzi school. Mr. Zongji has a family tradition of learning, extensive knowledge across various fields, and has been repeatedly recommended for official positions based on Confucian learning. However, due to his mother's advanced age, he chose not to take up such duties and instead devoted himself to literature. I have engaged in discussions with him and read his works, finding them as vast as an arsenal of military knowledge and as rich as a treasure trove of wisdom. They contain nothing but astonishing wonders and rare treasures of the world; this compilation is merely one item among many in such a vast collection. Therefore, those who read this compilation may not fully appreciate the breadth of Mr. Zongji's learning. In the month of August in the fourteenth year of the Hongwu era, Wu Zhi wrote this preface at the Jinde Hall of the school in Qiantang.

Preface to *New Tales of the Lamp Snipping* I have observed that Master Han of Changli composed *The Biography of Mao Ying*, and Liu Zihou read it and found it extraordinary, saying it was like capturing dragons and snakes, wrestling tigers and leopards, and engaging them in fierce combat, yet he had no time to spare for such pursuits. Ancient writers would praise each other in this manner. Later, when Liu Zihou wrote *The Exiled Dragon* and *The Biography of the River Intendant*, I have not heard of anyone criticizing him for being frivolous or licentious. Is it that the vision of our predecessors was not as sharp as that of today? Or is it simply a reflection of their sincere and generous spirit? My friend Qu Zongji composed *New Tales of the Lamp Snipping*, and the strange tales he recorded are even more fantastic than those told by Ma Ruzi, though in terms of licentiousness, none surpasses the *River Intendant*. Yet some still take delight in finding amusement in it, lamenting how unrefined and modern the customs have become. Qu Zongji, with his principles of praising goodness and condemning evil, immersed himself in the world of literature and writing during his teaching. Having heard and seen much, his knowledge has grown richer. Fearing that over time, he might forget these things, he selected stories that could inspire or serve as a warning, compiled them into a collection, and kept them in his chest for his own pleasure. This is the intention of Qu Zongji. I, being less intelligent, do not know whether it is right or wrong, nor do I know what is praiseworthy and what is laughable. Upon reading it, I see that it contains prose, poetry, essays, lyrics, things to be pleased with, things to be sad about, things to be frightened by, and things to be laughed at. It is clear that Qu Zongji is skilled in literature and has the ability to write other works as well. Qu Zongji asked me to write a preface, so I composed an ancient-style poem in response.

Talented individuals from Shanyang, who are your companions? Their words span the present and the past! In spring, the peach blossoms dye their temperaments; in autumn, the osmanthus flowers perfume their speech. Feeling the bittersweet encounters, their hearts beat rapidly; though it seems groundless, there is still a basis. In the dimly lit tent, they play the flute during the day; by the window, they trim the lamp warmly at night. Suddenly sunny, then suddenly rainy; they want to weep in sorrow, yet dance in joy.

The Jade Flute leans on the moon, playing the phoenix; golden bars are entwined with smoke, trapping a parrot. Who leaves a trace in the natural order? A single thought can move the heart. If one does not fail in doing good and avoiding evil, strange and unusual things may come to pass. A man who has not achieved his goals may be reduced to a lowly state, like a tiger becoming a dog; washing one's feet in clear water, removing several measures of mud, Cold and rigid, with a voice that sounds sharp and metallic, removing one's hat and looking around with eyes that shine like lightning. A Taoist practitioner moves like a green snake in the sky, cutting down ordinary "flower and moon demons," In the vast sea of dust, with countless bubbles, a lone pine tree in the clouds, with half a gourd of wine. All things in the world are like bubbles, but sometimes emotions or passions can endure. No one can truly know the depth of the Twelve Peaks of Wushan; a cloud-mother screen as thin as paper. The beauty of Yingying's house is described in detail; a low-hanging moon in a building where Yan Yan resides. Pines and cypresses have always had a solitary and upright character; not only can mandarin ducks live together, but other creatures can also do so. Having lived by the Qiantang River for a long time, one grows weary of seeing the tides come and go, Swallows bring spring back multiple times; broken dreams and fragmented spirits, where do they fall? Returning the book to the reader and singing a long song, desiring to drink from a golden cup, Falling asleep after drinking, a journey in a luxurious carriage, possibly a metaphor for a dream or a fleeting experience. Written by Gui Heng of Mu on June 6, 1399 (the fourth year of the Hongwu era of the Ming dynasty). Table of Contents Volume One of *Jian Deng Xin Hua* (New Tales of the Lamp) - *Shui Gong Qing Hui Lu* (Record of the Water Palace Celebration) - *San Shan Fu Di Zhi* (Records of the Three Mountains' Blessed Land) - *Hua Ting Feng Gu Ren Ji* (Encounter with an Old Friend in Hua Ting) - *Jin Feng Chai Ji* (The Golden Phoenix Hairpin) - *Lian Fang Lou Ji* (The Linked Fragrant Pavilion) Volume Two of *Jian Deng Xin Hua* (New Tales of the Lamp)

Records of the Dream of Mr. Linghu Records of Visiting the Recluses in TianTai The Record of Mr. Teng's Drunken Tour in the Garden of Gathered Scenery The Record of the Peony Lantern The Record of the Strange Encounter in WeiTang Volume Three of A New Tale of the Lamp-Cut The Annals of the Office of Prosperity and Ascension The Record of the Wild Temple in Yongzhou The Record of Shenyang Cave The Biography of Beloved Minister The Biography of Cui Cui Volume Four of A New Tale of the Lamp-Cut The Record of the Spirit Assembly in LongTang The Biography of the Judge of the Void The Biography of the Scholar of Literary Repair The Record of the Night Cruise on Jian Lake The Biography of the Green-Clothed Person Appendix The Record of the Autumn Fragrance Pavilion The Record of Sending Plum Blossoms Volume One of A New Tale of the Lamp-Cut The Record of the Celebration in the Palace of Water In the year of Jia Shen during the reign of Emperor Renzong, a scholar from Chaozhou named Yu Shanwen was sitting idly in his home during the day when suddenly two strong men, wearing yellow turbans and embroidered robes, entered from outside and paid their respects before him, saying, "We are here on behalf of Prince Guangli to invite you." Shanwen was startled and replied, "Prince Guangli is the deity of the sea, while I am but a mere mortal. The paths of the living and the dead are vastly different; how could we possibly meet?" The two men said, "Please just go with us, there is no need to refuse." So they accompanied him out of the south gate, where they saw a large red boat moored by the riverbank. They boarded the boat, and two yellow dragons carried it forward at the speed of wind and rain, reaching their destination in an instant. The boat stopped at the gate, and the two men went inside to report. Shortly after, they invited Shanwen to enter. Prince Guangli descended the steps to greet him, saying, "I have long admired your reputation and have humbly requested your presence. I hope you will not be surprised." He then invited Shanwen up the steps and they sat facing each other.

Shanwen, with a hesitant and deferential demeanor, declined the invitation. Guangli said, "You reside in the world of the living, while I dwell in the realm of the waters. We do not have a hierarchical relationship, so there is no need for you to refuse." Shanwen replied, "Your Majesty is of great importance, but I am merely a humble scholar. How dare I accept such a great honor!" He firmly declined. Two officials on either side of Guangli, named Yuan Canjun and Bie Zhubu, stepped forward and said, "The guest's words are correct. Your Majesty should grant his request. It would be unwise to diminish your authority and lose the respect of others." Guangli then sat in the center, and had a separate seat placed to his right, instructing Shanwen to sit. He then said, "My humble abode is remote and secluded, surrounded by dragons and crocodiles, and inhabited by fish and crabs. It is difficult to demonstrate divine power and proclaim imperial decrees. Now, I wish to construct a new hall named Lingde. The craftsmen have been gathered, and the materials are ready. The only thing lacking is the beam inscription. I have heard that you possess extraordinary talent and strategies for saving the world, so I have specially invited you here to write it for me." Immediately, he ordered his attendants to fetch a white jade inkstone, a writing brush made of rhinoceros horn, and a piece of fine silk fabric, placing them before Shanwen. Shanwen bowed and followed orders, finishing the inscription in one stroke without making any corrections. The words read: "O Great Spirit, you who dwell in the depths of the waters, your power is vast and your wisdom profound. You who command the creatures of the sea, your presence is awe-inspiring. Now, as we build this hall of Spiritual Virtue, may it serve as a testament to your divine majesty. May it stand as a beacon of your glory, a place where the world may come to seek your favor. We, your humble servants, offer this inscription with reverence and gratitude, hoping that it will bring you honor and that our endeavors may be blessed by your divine presence." Guangli was pleased with the inscription and praised Shanwen for his talent and skill. The new hall of Lingde was soon completed, and it became a place of great importance in the realm of the waters.

Considering the vastness of the world, the sea is the greatest; within the realm of humans and deities, the gods are the most spiritual. Since it is a place of worship, can it lack the grandeur of the temple hall? Therefore, the treasure hall is rebuilt, and a new splendid name is unveiled; dragon bones are used as beams, and divine light shines like the sun; fish-scale tiles are made, and auspicious qi coils in the sky. Curtains adorned with pearls and white jade are hung, and boats decorated with blue birds and yellow dragons are connected. When the intricately carved windows are opened, the sea color is visible at the door; when the embroidered doors are opened, the cloud shadows appear at the veranda. Favorable weather conditions stabilize the southern sea for over eight thousand miles; the vastness of heaven and earth endures for billions of years. It connects the tributaries of the Yangtze and Han rivers, receiving offerings from streams and lakes. The Heavenly Fish and Purple Phoenix arrive in a flurry; the demons of the Ghost Country and the Rakshasas come in turn. It stands firm like the Lingguang of Lu, beautiful like the Jingfu of Han. It controls the southern regions and guides the eastern regions, always enhancing the grand view; it calls out to the gates of heaven and presents lapis lazuli, inspiring good praise. Therefore, a short song is composed to assist in raising the beam. Throwing the beam to the east, the mythical islands of Fangzhang and Penglai are in sight. Looking at the mythical tree of the sun standing three hundred feet tall, with the golden rooster's cry signaling the rise of the sun, the temple's spiritual and cultural significance is evident.

The West of Throw-Bridge, the weak river flows through the desert, the path is not lost. The Queen Mother of the West descends in her splendid attire from the Jade Pool, and a pair of green birds sing towards people. To the South of Throw-Bridge, the vast waters spread out, containing countless tribes. To know the width of the boundaries, how far does the great Peng bird fly until the water turns blue? To the North of Throw-Bridge, the stars shine brightly around the North Star. Looking far into the distance, where is the Central Plains? A single line of green mountains floats with a green hue. Above Throw-Bridge, riding a dragon at night to accompany the heavenly guard. After presenting a letter in the sleeve, it is all for the benefit of all living beings, removing calamities and miasmas. Below Throw-Bridge, the aquatic creatures are diverse and receive the grace of the divine. In the early morning, the sounds of praise and worship are frequent, as the River Gods and Lake Lords attend the divine carriage. May it be wished that after raising the ridge beam, all tribes return to benevolence, and all spirits revere virtue. The palaces of pearls and shell gates should reflect the three luminaries, and the dragon robes and embroidered skirts should be prepared for the five blessings. After writing, the document was presented. Guangli was greatly pleased. A day was chosen to settle in the camp, and envoys were sent to the three seas in the east, west, and north, inviting their kings to attend the celebration hall meeting. The next day, the three gods arrived, accompanied by thousands of chariots and tens of thousands of horses. Divine dolphins and toxic sea monsters leap and jump, long whales and great fish gallop left and right, soldiers with fish heads and ghost faces hold flags and wield spears and halberds, and their numbers are countless. On that day, Guangli wore a crown that reaches the sky, a robe of fine silk, and a tablet of green jade, hastening to welcome them at the door, the ceremony being very solemn. The three gods also wore their respective crowns, their swords and belts strict, their majesty extremely dignified and respectful, but the colors of their robes varied according to their directions. After exchanging greetings, they bowed and yielded, and then sat down.

Shanwen, dressed in plain clothes, sat at the corner of the hall. He was just about to exchange greetings with the three deities when suddenly, behind the seat of King Guangyuan of the East Sea, there was a court official with a hat of iron and long mustaches, known as Redyuan Gong. He leaped out in front of Guangli and asked, "This grand hall has now been completed, and this gathering is specially arranged for the three kings. Even the leaders of the Yangtze and Han rivers, and the kings of lakes and marshes, cannot participate in the banquet. The etiquette here is truly strict. What kind of person is that one dressed in plain clothes sitting at the end? How dare he be so bold?" Guangli replied, "This is Shanwen, a talented scholar from Chaoyang. I built the Lingde Hall and asked him to write the beam-lifting text, so I kept him here." Guangyuan immediately said, "With a scholar present, why are you saying so much? Retreat for now!" Redyuan Gong then blushed and stepped down. Soon after, the wine was served and the music began. Twenty beautiful women, shaking their bright pendants and dragging their light skirts, danced in front of the banquet, performing the Lingbo dance and singing the Lingbo lyrics: If there is someone in the waves, picking willows and plucking lotus flowers. Shaking jade rings and crystal pendants, ringing and tinkling melodiously. Dressed gracefully like a startled wild goose, their bodies agile like a swimming dragon. Light dust rises under their silk socks, the slanting sun illuminates their beautiful faces. Standing alone, they move back and forth, their grace is uncontainable and untraceable. Suddenly they float away, riding on the cool, light wind.

After the dance concluded, forty young singers appeared, dressed in fresh makeup and perfumed sleeves, performing a dance and singing a song about picking lotus flowers: On fragrant boats made of cinnamomum and orchid, sailing on the shimmering waves, far away on a journey. Throwing away my jade ring at the parting shore, unfastening my jade pendant at the fragrant island. The boat sways in the waves, uncertain and unstable, breaking off the lotus flower and cutting the stem. Why should the dew wet my clothes? Why should the wind blow through my hair? The oarsman's song rises, and the colorful sleeves are waved, the jade green birds scatter, and the mandarin ducks fly. Spreading lotus leaves as a canopy, weaving lotus threads into clothing. The sun is setting, and the wind is getting stronger; a light smoke rises, and a faint moon appears. Return early, it is hard to stay long; enjoying the beauty, happiness cannot be fully realized. After the two dances were completed, then the magical turtle drums were struck, and the jade dragon flutes were blown, all the music was presented, and wine glasses and chopsticks were mixed together. Thus, the three gods of east, west, and north, together holding a cup, presented it to the good man before him and said, "We live in remote corners, unaware of ceremonies; today's gathering allows us to witness a grand ceremony, and it is fortunate to have a great gentleman present, greatly enhancing the splendor. We wish to compose a poem to commemorate this, so that it may be passed down to the dragon palaces and water realms, which would also be a splendid event. Do you not think so?" The good man could not refuse, and thus presented a poem celebrating the gathering in twenty rhyming couplets: The imperial virtue is vast as heaven and earth, the divine power ensures peace in the mountains and seas. The deep palace has boats and buildings, the waterways are calm. Titles and ranks of nobility are bestowed, territories are wide and expansive. The divine power is known far and wide, achievements are complete and secure. Messages from the southern pole are always conveyed, officials are appointed in the southern regions.

Ascending the hall to receive jade and silk, a banquet is held for the gentry. The phoenix dances in three-tiered cups, the dragon carries a saddle adorned with seven treasures. Messages are delivered by leaping carp, six sea turtles support the carriage. The Queen Mother stirs a golden cauldron, the Heavenly Consort holds a jade plate. Cups made of red amber, sleeves brushing against green jade-like stones. On the seat, Lady Xiang Ling dances, frequently playing the lute. At the end of the performance, a maiden from the Han dynasty arrives, holding a green flag. Auspicious mist obscures pearl curtains, auspicious smoke curls around painted railings. Screens made of mica gleam, curtains of crystal are drawn back. Together, they drink the dew of the Three Dangers, eat the Nine Transformations Pill. A good time is fit for intoxication, pleasant events are worthy of lingering. Exotic flavors fill the mouth, spiritual light illuminates the inner organs. As if one had reached the Tushita Heaven, or as if dreaming in Handan. Offering wine and singing on a high platform, joy and festivity are fully enjoyed. Writing poetry to record glorious events, spring colors fill the brush. The poem is presented, the audience is delighted. Soon, the sun sets in the western sea, the moon rises in the eastern valley, the gods become drunk and stagger out, each returning to their realm, the sound of chariots and horses lined up in abundance still echoing after a while. The next day, a special banquet is held to thank the skilled writer. After the banquet, a glass dish holds ten night-lighting pearls and two sky-reaching rhinoceros horns, as a reward for the writer, two envoys are sent to return him to the prefecture. The writer arrives home, selling the treasures in a Persian jewelry shop, gaining wealth in the tens of thousands, becoming a wealthy family. Later, he no longer cares about fame and fortune, abandons his family to seek the Dao, wandering through famous mountains, his ultimate fate unknown.

**Records of the Three Mountains as Auspicious Places** Mr. Zizhi was from Shandong. He was born with a dull nature and unable to understand poetry and books. His family was quite wealthy, and they engaged in managing estates. There was a Mr. Miao in the same village who was appointed to an official position in Fujian but lacked travel expenses. He borrowed two hundred taels of silver from Mr. Zizhi. Mr. Zizhi, trusting their relationship as fellow villagers, did not ask for a written document and lent the full amount. At the end of the Zhizheng era, Shandong was in chaos, and Mr. Zizhi was robbed by a band of thieves, leaving his family's wealth completely depleted. At that time, Chen Youding was holding Fujian, and the seven regions of Fujian were relatively peaceful. Mr. Zizhi then took his son and traveled by sea to Fuzhou, intending to visit Mr. Miao and seek his protection. When they arrived, Mr. Miao was indeed serving under Chen Youding, holding power and influence, with great authority and prestige, and a prominent and influential household. Mr. Zizhi was very happy, but after the hardships, he had traveled long and arduous journeys, wearing tattered clothes and looking haggard and worn out, so he did not dare to see Mr. Miao immediately. Instead, he rented a house in the city, settled his wife and children, dressed up his attire, and chose a day to go. Just as Mr. Miao was about to leave, Mr. Zizhi bowed at the head of the horse. At first, Mr. Miao seemed not to recognize him, but when they talked about their hometown and introduced their names, Mr. Miao was surprised and thanked him. He then invited Mr. Zizhi into the room and treated him with the courtesy of a host and guest. After a long time, they drank tea and then parted. The next day, Mr. Zizhi went again, and they had only three cups of wine and fruit. Mr. Miao treated him coldly and without concern, and he did not mention the matter of the silver.

Zi Shi returned home, staying in a desolate and lonely place. His wife and children complained and scolded him, saying, "You traveled thousands of miles to seek help, what did you do? Now you are sold by three cups of thin wine, even if you didn't say a word, what do we hope for?" Zi Shi had no choice but to visit again the next day. The recipient seemed to have become tired of him. As Zi Shi was about to speak, the recipient suddenly said, "I remember with deep gratitude the loan of travel expenses you gave me before. However, as a low-ranking official in a poor condition with a meager salary, how could I betray your kindness? I hope to repay with a promissory note, and I will repay gradually in full." Zi Shi was startled and said, "We are from the same hometown, and we have known each other since childhood with a close bond. I received your help in times of need, and there was never a written agreement. Why are you saying this now?" The recipient said seriously, "The promissory note is still there, but I am afraid after the war, you may have lost it. However, whether the note exists or not, I do not care. I only hope for a longer repayment period so that I can make efforts." Zi Shi nodded and left, finding his words exaggerated and false, betraying his kindness like this. He was in a dilemma, unable to move forward or backward. After half a month, he visited again, but the recipient received him with warm words, finally giving no money. Repeatedly evading and delaying, until half a year later. There was a small temple in the market, on the way to the recipient's residence, where Zi Shi often rested at the door. The temple's owner, Master Xuan Yuan, was a wise man. Seeing him come and go for a long time, he talked with him, and they became familiar.

As the last month of winter approached, the new year was drawing near. Self-Realized, living in poverty and boredom, visited Master Miao's residence and knelt, weeping, as he said, "The new year is almost here, and my wife and children are suffering from hunger and cold. My purse lacks even a single coin, and my jar has no stored grain. The silver I received before, I dare not ask for now. I only wish to give a measure of water to revive a fish stranded in a dried-up ditch, or offer a pot of food to save someone starving in the shade of a mulberry tree. This would be a favor from an old friend. I humbly beg for your pity, compassion, and help!" He then prostrated himself on the ground. Master Miao helped him up, counted the days on his fingers, and said, "In ten more days, it will be New Year's Eve. You can stay at home and wait. I will send two measures of grain and two ingots of silver. I will have someone deliver them to your house as funds for the new year. Please do not be surprised by the small amount." And he repeatedly warned him, "Do not leave your home to wait." Self-Realized thanked him and left, returning home to comfort his wife and children with what Master Miao had said. On that day, the whole family anxiously awaited. Self-Realized sat calmly on the bed, sending someone to the gate to watch for the delivery. Soon, the messenger ran in and said, "Someone is carrying grain here!" He rushed out to meet them, but they passed by his house without stopping. Self-Realized still thought the deliverers did not recognize his home and hurried to ask them. They said, "This is from Mr. Zhang, who is sending it to his guest." He silently returned. Soon after, the young child came in and reported, "Someone is bringing money!" He rushed out to greet them, but they passed by his door without entering. He stopped them and asked, "This is from County Magistrate Li, who is sending it to his guest." He looked dejected and felt ashamed.

In this manner, it happened several times. By evening, he finally completely cut off all traces. The next day was New Year's Day, but he was misled by the situation, and basic necessities like grains and firewood were not available, causing his wife and children to cry facing each other. Zi Shi could not bear his anger any longer and secretly sharpened his blade, sitting and waiting for dawn. At dawn, he directly went to the door of Mr. Miao, intending to wait for him to come out and stab him. At that time, the morning had not yet opened, and the road was empty. Suddenly, dozens of ghosts with strange shapes and forms appeared behind him, some holding swords and spears, others holding hammers and chisels, bareheaded and naked, with a very fierce appearance. In a moment, Zi Shi returned, followed by over a hundred gentlemen with golden crowns and jade pendants, some hitting the canopy, others raising the banners, with gentle demeanor and pleasant expressions, the intention very calm. The elderly man,轩辕翁, was unpredictable and thought that Zi Shi had died. After finishing reciting the scriptures, he hurried to visit Zi Shi, only to find that Zi Shi was still alive. Sitting down,轩辕翁 asked, "Today's morning, where were you going? Why did you leave so quickly and return so slowly? I would like to hear about it." Zi Shi dared not hide and spoke in detail: "Mr. Miao's injustice has made me so miserable! This morning, I secretly sharpened my blade, intending to go and kill him to satisfy my anger. When I arrived at his door, I suddenly thought to myself: 'He has indeed wronged me, but why should my wife and children suffer? Moreover, my mother is old and needs support. If I kill him, his family will have no one to rely on. Rather let others wrong me, than I wrong others.' So I restrained myself and returned."

Laoxuanyuan heard this and bowed respectfully, congratulating, "Your son will have future rewards; the divine has already known." Zishí asked for the reason. The old man said, "A single thought of evil brings forth an evil spirit; a single thought of goodness brings forth a benevolent spirit. It is like a shadow that follows its form, or an echo that responds to sound. Therefore, one must not harbor evil in one's heart, nor commit sins that harm virtue, even in secret or in haste." He then explained what he had seen and comforted him, giving him a small amount of money and rice to help him in his urgent need. However, Zishí remained gloomy and unhappy. By evening, he threw himself into the eight-cornered well at the foot of the Three Divine Mountains. The water suddenly parted, revealing stone walls on both sides as sharp as cut, with a narrow path inside, barely wide enough for a person to walk. Zishí felt his way along the wall, walking several hundred steps, until the path ended and he emerged at a small opening. There, the sky and earth were clear, the sun and moon shone down, and it was clearly a different world. He saw a grand palace, with golden characters on the signboard reading "Three Divine Mountains of Blessing."

Zi Shi entered the temple after gazing in. The long corridor was quiet during the day, and the incense from the ancient temple had dissipated. Wandering and looking around, there was no sign of anyone. Only the faint sound of the temple bells and drums could be heard, coming from afar. Hungry and weak, he could not move forward and lay tired beside the stone altar. Suddenly, a Taoist priest appeared, dragging a robe of blue clouds and shaking a jade pendant like a bright moon. He approached and called Zi Shi up, smiling and asking, "Do you know the taste of traveling, Scholar?" Zi Shi bowed and replied, "The taste of traveling, I have had enough of it. How mistaken the title of Scholar is!" The Taoist priest said, "Do you not remember drafting the imperial edict for the Western Regions in the Xing Sheng Temple?" Zi Shi replied, "I am a simple man from Shandong, a humble scholar. Forty years old, never having read a book, I have never traveled to the capital. How could there be such a thing as drafting an imperial edict?" The Taoist priest said, "You must be troubled by hunger, not having the time to remember past events." He then took out a few pears and jujubes from his sleeve and offered them to eat, saying, "These are called the pear of exchange and the jujube of fire. Eating them will allow you to know past and future events."

Upon finishing his meal, he became clearly awake and enlightened, remembering that he had once served as a scholar and drafted the West Famine Pool beside the Xingsheng Temple in the capital, as if it were yesterday. He then asked the Taoist priest, "What sin did I commit in my past life that I now suffer this retribution?" The priest replied, "You are not guilty. But while in office, you held your literary talent in high regard and did not help promote younger scholars, so in this life you are ignorant and illiterate; you held your position in high regard and did not accept wandering scholars, so in this life you are wandering without support. " Zi Shi then pointed to a high-ranking official of the present day and asked, "This person is the Chancellor. He is never satisfied with his greed, openly practices bribery, and what punishment will he face in the future?" The priest said, "He is the king of insatiable greed, with ten furnaces in the underworld to cast his ill-gotten wealth, and now his fortune is full, so he will suffer the disaster of imprisonment. " He asked again, "This person is the Pingzhang, and he does not restrain his soldiers, killing innocent people. What punishment will he face in the future?" The priest said, "He is the king of many killings, with three hundred dark soldiers, all with copper heads and iron foreheads, to assist him in his cruelty. Now his fate has declined, and he will suffer the calamity of being cut up."

"Another person asked, 'If someone is a high-ranking official responsible for overseeing local administration, but fails to enforce the law; if someone is a prefectural governor, but taxes and labor services are not evenly distributed; if someone is an official responsible for pacifying and consoling local areas, but does not know what is being announced; if someone is a high-ranking military or administrative official, but does not know which direction is being conquered, then what kind of punishment should they receive?' The Taoist replied, 'All of these individuals have already been subjected to handcuffs and shackles, their necks bound with chains, their bodies reduced to rotten flesh and filthy bones, awaiting the fate of their souls after execution. What is there to count?' Zi Shi then mentioned the matter of Miao Jun's debt. The Taoist said, 'He is merely the storekeeper of General Wang; how can his property be moved without permission?' The Taoist then added, 'Within three years, the world will undergo a transformation, great disaster will come, and it is very frightening. You should choose a place to live, otherwise you might share the calamity of the fish in the pond.' Zi Shi asked for a place to avoid the war."

The Taoist priest said, "Fuqing is acceptable." He then said, "It is not as good as Funing." After speaking, he told Zi Shi, "You have been here for a long time, your family is anxiously waiting, you may return now." Zi Shi informed him that there was no way out, and the Taoist priest pointed a path for him to leave. He then bowed twice and took his leave. After walking about two miles, he emerged from a cave at the back of the mountain and arrived home, but it had been half a month. He hurriedly took his wife and children directly to the village of Funing, where he cultivated land and tended a garden to make a living. While swinging the hoe, a metallic sound was heard, and he found four ingots of silver. His family then gradually became more prosperous. Later, Zhang seized the seal, the Grand Secretary was detained, the army approached the city, Chen was captured, other officials could not protect their heads, and Master Miao was killed by General Wang, whose family assets were all taken. Counting by years, it was only three years, and the Taoist's words were all proven true. A Record of Meeting an Old Friend in Huating There were two scholars named Quan and Jia from Songjiang. Both were rich in literature, free-spirited, fond of wine, and unconcerned with minor details. They often took on the role of a wandering knight. During the late Yuan Dynasty, Zhang held power in Zhejiang West, and Songjiang was a subordinate prefecture. The two scholars came and went among the powerful, speaking grandly and arguing forcefully, as if no one else was present. Wealthy families and powerful clans welcomed them, fearing to be left behind. Quan wrote a poem: Gray hair crowns the head, feeling the two hairs. The west wind penetrates the phoenix robe with its chill. Looking up at the sky, he dared not exhale deeply, Transforming into a rainbow ten thousand feet high.

The Scholar had a poem that went: Across the land, the turmoil of war has not ceased, How could a scholar be suited to live in seclusion in the mountains and forests! In my sleeve, I carry a sword of Qilin, Supporting half of the southeastern sky. His poems were generally of this kind, and people further believed in his self-confidence. In the first year of the Wu dynasty, the national army besieged Suzhou, but it was not yet conquered. A person from Shangyang named Qian He Gao raised an army to support the Zhang clan. The two sons compared themselves to Yan Zhuang and Shang Rang, and they carried their staff to the door of the Zhang clan, participating in their plans, and then captured Jiaxing and other counties. Not long after, the army was defeated, and they all drowned. In the fourth year of the Hongwu era, a scholar from Huating named Shi Ruoxu had an errand in the suburbs. He had always been friendly with the two sons, and he suddenly encountered them on the road. They were accompanied by servants who were trying to save people, and their demeanor was just as it had been before. They greeted Shi Ruoxu and said, "Shi Jun, are you well?" Shi Ruoxu forgot that they were already dead, and he bowed and conversed with them, and they sat on mats of pine branches. They talked for a long time. One of them suddenly sighed deeply and said, "Zhuge Changmin once said, 'Those in poverty often think of wealth, and those in wealth often face danger.' This statement is not a definitive truth. If one desires wealth, how can one avoid danger? How can there be a crane in Yangzhou? A man cannot leave a good name for a hundred generations, but he should leave a bad name for ten thousand years. Liu Heti, after becoming the King of Han Dong, spoke these words on his deathbed: 'I was originally at home tilling the fields, but I was misled by the high-minded and virtuous ones to this point!' What a vulgar statement, it is enough to make people laugh for a thousand years!" The Scholar said, "Liu Heti is not worth mentioning! Like Han's Tianheng and Tang's Li Mi, they too can be considered shining stars in iron."

Xiang Yu, at the beginning, ruled alongside Liu Bang (汉祖) as a king (南面称孤), ashamed to submit as a vassal (耻更称臣), fled to the island of Hui (诲岛), where he could have died, but was deceived by the words of great kings and petty princes (眩于大王小侯之语), and died while traveling to the eastern capital (东都). Li Mi (密) led a rebellion, and the founder of the Tang Dynasty (唐祖) sent a letter congratulating him, proclaiming him as the leader of an alliance (推为盟主), but after his defeat and retreat into the Guanzhong region (及兵败入关), he hoped to be appointed as a high-ranking official (乃望以台司见处), showing his lack of wisdom (其无知识如此)! A man of integrity dies when he must (大丈夫死即死矣), how can one bear to live by breathing on someone's throat (何忍向人喉下取气耶)? Han Xin (韩信) established the Han Dynasty (建炎汉之业), but finally suffered execution (卒受诛夷); Liu Wenjing (刘文静) initiated the rise of the Tang Dynasty from Jinyang (启晋阳之祚), but finally suffered humiliation (终加戮辱). Even their meritorious ministers were like this (彼之功臣尚尔), what about others (于他人何有哉)! "Luo Binwang (骆宾王) assisted Li Jingye (李敬业) in a rebellion, drafting a declaration exposing the evils of the Wu family (檄武氏之恶), and after his defeat, he was able to live in leisure at Lingyin Temple, composing verses praising the fragrance of osmanthus flowers (咏桂子天香之句). Huang Chao (黄巢) disrupted the Tang Dynasty, deserving punishment, but after his defeat, he shaved his head and dressed in monk's robes (削发被缁), escaping his pursuers, and wrote a poem: 'Having worn out his armor, he now wears monk's robes (铁衣着尽着僧衣).' If these two men, as the chief villains (身为首恶), could ultimately escape disaster (终能脱祸), their wisdom and strategy were indeed profound (可谓智术之深矣)." Jia (贾) laughed and said, "If it is indeed so, we should be ashamed of ourselves!" Quan (全) quickly replied, "The old people have fallen (故人在坠), there's no need to discuss other matters, it only adds to sadness (徒增伤感尔)." He then unbuttoned the green fur coat he was wearing (解所御绿裘), instructing his servant to exchange it for wine in the nearby village (令仆于近村质酒而饮). When the wine arrived, they drank several rounds, and Ruo Xu (若虚) asked the two men, "Your writings, widely spoken of (播在人口), today's gathering, can we not have a fine work to commemorate it (可无佳制以记之乎)?" After thinking for a while, Quan's poem was completed first, and he immediately recited it:

For several years, war has connected the ends of the earth, and we have spent our years among heaps of bones. Du Yu, with his grievances, wept blood, and Deng You, without children, could not continue his family line. At the time, they boasted like pigs of Liaodong, but today they have become frogs at the bottom of a well. Who is the master of this spring scene, as wildflowers bloom amidst thorns and sand? In the vast, desolate wilderness, birds fly chaotically, and people and cities sigh at the change. The bones of the dead are buried in the sand, why bother to bury them, and the souls stained with blood cannot return. No one makes cold food offerings, and the coarse robe weeps tears under the slanting evening light. All of us living are scattered like this, and the only regret is that my lifelong ambitions were thwarted. After reciting, Ruoxu was shocked and said, "The two gentlemen usually recited very freely, but today's work, how much more sorrowful than usual, how different from before?" They looked at each other in silence, but sighed deeply and let out a few long cries. Soon, the wine ran out, and they bid farewell and left. Walking a dozen steps, they saw nothing, and Ruoxu was shocked, finally realizing that he had been dead for a long time. They only saw the forest edges shrouded in smoke, and the mountain peaks bathed in sunset, with only crows and magpies chirping in the dense undergrowth. They hurried to a wine shop in the nearby village, checked the ledger, and examined the fur coat used as collateral, which crumbled to pieces upon touch, like butterfly wings caught in the wind. Ruoxu stayed overnight at the wine shop and hurried back the next morning. After this, he dared not pass this way again.

**The Gold Phoenix Hairpin Record** During the Da De period, a wealthy merchant named Wu lived next to the Spring Wind Pavilion in Yangzhou, and he was a close friend of Cui, an official. Wu had a daughter named Xingniang, and Cui had a son named Xingge, both of whom were infants. Cui asked Wu to let his daughter marry his son, and Wu agreed, using a gold phoenix hairpin as a symbol of the promise. Soon after, Cui went on a distant official tour and did not communicate for fifteen years. Xingniang, now living in the inner chambers, was nineteen years old. Her mother said to Wu, "Cui's son has been away for fifteen years without any news, and Xingniang has grown up. We cannot keep to our previous words and let her lose her prime." Wu replied, "I have already promised my old friend, and the agreement is already made. How could I go back on my word?" Xingniang, hoping for her husband's return, fell ill and lay bedridden for half a year before passing away. Her parents mourned deeply.

At the time of her death, her mother held a gold phoenix hairpin and wept over her body, saying, "This is something from your husband's family. Now that you are gone, what use would I have for this?" She then placed the hairpin in her hair and buried her. Two months later, Cui arrived. The defense official welcomed him warmly and inquired about the reason for his visit. Cui replied, "My father was a magistrate of Xuande Prefecture and has passed away, and my mother also died several years ago. I have now completed the mourning period and have traveled a long distance to reach here." The defense official shed tears and said, "Xing娘 had a short life. She fell ill because she thought of you and died with regret two months ago. She has been buried." He then led Cui into the room, to the table before her spirit tablet, and burned paper money to inform her. The whole family wailed in grief. The defense official said to Cui, "Your parents have both passed away, and the journey was long. Now that you are here, you can stay and eat at our house. The son of an old friend is like my own son. Do not treat yourself as an outsider because Xing娘 has died."

Immediately, he was ordered to pack his belongings and settle in a small study near the gate. Nearly half a month had passed. It was Qingming Festival, and because of the death of a female member, the entire family went to the ancestral tomb. Xing娘 had a sister named Qing娘, who was seventeen years old, and she also went along that day. Only Sheng was left at home to guard the house. By evening, they returned, and the sky was already dark. Sheng stood on the left side of the gate to greet them. There were two carriages; the first had already entered, and the second approached Sheng. It seemed as though something had fallen to the ground, making a clinking sound. Sheng waited for the carriage to pass, then quickly picked it up and found it to be a gold phoenix hairpin. He wanted to return it inside, but the inner door was already closed, and he could not enter. So he returned to the small study and sat alone with a lit candle. He thought about the failure of his marriage and felt alone and miserable, realizing that staying as a guest was not a long-term plan. He sighed several times. Just as he was about to go to bed, he suddenly heard the sound of someone knocking on the door. When he asked, no one answered. Soon, the knocking continued, repeating three times. He then opened the door and saw a beautiful young woman standing outside. When she saw the door open, she quickly pulled her skirt and entered. Sheng was greatly surprised. The woman spoke softly and discreetly to him, saying, "Don't you recognize me, sir? I am Qing娘, the sister of Xing娘. Earlier, I dropped my hairpin from the carriage, did you pick it up?" She immediately led Sheng to bed. Sheng, considering the kindness of the host, declined, saying, "I dare not."

The girl rejected him very severely, up to three times. Suddenly, she blushed and angrily said, "My father treats you with the courtesy of a son or nephew, and has entrusted you to me. Yet you lured me here in the middle of the night. What are you planning to do? I will report this to my father and sue you in court, and I will certainly not let you go." The man was afraid and, having no choice, agreed to her demands. By dawn, he left. From then on, he would secretly enter at dusk and leave at dawn, coming and going between her room and a small study by the gate, for more than a month and a half. One evening, she said to him, "I live in the inner chambers, and you reside outside. Today's events were fortunate in that no one noticed. But I fear that good things often have many obstacles, and happy times can be easily interrupted. If our actions become known, my father will punish us, and we will be locked up like a parrot in a cage, or startled like a pair of ducks. Although I am willing to endure this, I am afraid it might tarnish your reputation. It would be better to act before it happens, carry something valuable and flee, or hide in a remote village, or conceal ourselves in a different region, so that we may live together in peace and harmony until old age, and avoid being separated." The man agreed with her plan and said, "Your words have reason, and I am thinking about it." He then thought to himself, feeling lonely and pitiful, with only a few close friends, that although he wanted to escape, where could he go? He remembered his father telling him that there was an old servant named Jin Rong, a man of integrity and righteousness, living in Lücheng, Zhenjiang, who engaged in farming as his occupation. Now, he would go to him, hoping he would not reject him.

By the fifth drumbeat of the night, the man and the woman went out lightly dressed, bought a boat, and crossed to Guazhou, rushing to Danyang. They inquired among the villagers and indeed found one named Jin Rong, whose family was very wealthy, and who served as the village headman. The man was overjoyed and went directly to his door. Upon arrival, they did not recognize each other at first. The man told him his father's name, title, and hometown, as well as his own nickname, and only then did Jin Rong remember and recognize him. He set up a seat and mourned his master, holding the man and bowing before the seat, saying, "This is our family's young master." The man fully informed him of the reason, and Jin Rong vacated the main hall and lived there, treating him as if he were the old master. All his needs for clothing and food were provided abundantly. The man lived with Jin Rong's family for nearly a year. The woman told the man, "At first, I feared my parents' reprimand, so I fled with you like Zuo, out of necessity. Now, the old grain has been consumed, and the new grain has been harvested. Time flows like water, and it has already been a year. Moreover, the love for one's child is a universal human sentiment. If I return on my own, they will be happy to see me again and will not punish me. After all, my parents gave birth to me, and their kindness is immeasurable. How could there be a reason to sever ties forever? Why not go and see them?" The man agreed and went with her across the river into the city. As they approached his home, she said to the man, "I have been in hiding for a year. Now, if we suddenly go together, perhaps we will encounter their anger. You should go first to see, and I will moor the boat here and wait."

On the eve of his departure, the man was called back again, and a golden phoenix hairpin was given to him, with the words: "If there is any doubt or refusal, show this to them, and it will be acceptable." Upon reaching the door, the defense heard of this and came out to greet him, returning to express gratitude, saying, "Yesterday's care was inadequate, causing you to be uncomfortable in your place, and you had another place to go. It is my fault as an old man. Please do not take it amiss!" The man knelt and bowed to the ground, dared not look up, and only said, "Death penalty," with words that did not stop. The defense said, "What crime have I committed? Hastily said this. Please allow me to explain, relieve my doubts." The man then stood up and said, "In the past, the matter in the bedroom was secret, and there was much affection between us. I bear the name of being unjust, and I violated the law of private intercourse. I married without informing anyone, stole and fled, hid in a village, delayed time, long separated by distance and silence, letters and messages did not reach. Our feelings were deep between husband and wife, but how could I forget the kindness of my parents? Now I carefully bring your beloved daughter, together to return to your parents' home. I humbly hope you will understand our deep feelings, forgive our grave sins, so that we can live together until old age, forever happy and free. You have the kindness of indulgent love, and I have the joy of a suitable home. This is what I hope for, only hope for mercy." The defense, upon hearing this, was surprised and said, "My daughter is lying ill in bed, refusing even porridge, turning and needing help. How could there be such a thing?"

The father, fearing that accepting the proposal would bring shame to the family, used polite words to refuse it. He then said, "At present, Qingniang is in the boat. You can have someone carry her here." Despite his skepticism, the father sent a servant to check, but upon arrival, the servant saw nothing. The father then angrily scolded Cui Sheng, calling him superstitious and absurd. Cui Sheng, however, pulled out a golden phoenix hairpin from his sleeve and presented it to the father. Upon seeing the hairpin, the father was greatly surprised and exclaimed, "This is the hairpin my deceased daughter Xingniang was buried with! How did it come to be here?" While still in doubt, Qingniang suddenly rose from her bed, walked up to the front hall, and bowed to her father, saying, "Unfortunate Xingniang left her parents early and was abandoned in a remote place. However, my bond with Cui Lang is not broken. I have come here today with no other intention than to continue the marriage with my beloved sister Qingniang. If you agree, the illness will immediately be cured; if you do not listen to me, my life will end here." The whole family was shocked, as they saw the body of Qingniang but her speech and behavior were that of Xingniang. The father questioned her, "You are already dead, how can you be back in the world causing such confusion?" She replied, "I was not detained in the underworld because I was innocent. I was under the command of a noble lady, in charge of delivering messages. Since my bond in this life is not yet finished, I was given a year off to settle this bond with Cui Lang."

When the father heard her words with sincerity, he finally agreed. He then composed himself, bowed in thanks, and, taking Cui Sheng’s hand, wept bitterly as they parted. He said, “My parents have given their consent! You have always been a spoiled guest; be careful not to forget the old love for the new one.” After speaking these words, she wept uncontrollably and collapsed to the ground. When they looked at her, she was dead. They hurriedly gave her soup and medicine, but after some time she regained consciousness. Her illness had passed, and she moved as usual. When asked about previous events, she did not remember them at all, as though waking from a dream. They then chose an auspicious day to proceed with Cui Sheng’s marriage. Moved by Xing Niang’s affection, Cui Sheng sold her hairpin in the market, obtaining twenty ingots of silver. He used all of it to buy incense, candles, and paper money, and brought them to the Qionghua Temple, ordering the Taoist priests to conduct a three-day and three-night ritual to thank her. She appeared to him again in a dream and said, “I am grateful for your recommendation and elevation, and still have lingering feelings. Though separated by the realms of the living and the dead, I truly feel deeply indebted to you. My younger sister is gentle; you should treat her well.” Cui Sheng awoke in shock and sorrow. From then on, the dreams ceased. Alas, what a strange thing!

The Record of the Lian Fang Pavilion In Wu Prefecture, there was a wealthy family surnamed Xue, living in the early years of the Zhi Zheng era, outside the Changle Gate, engaged in the business of selling rice. They had two daughters, the elder named Lan Ying and the younger named Hui Ying, both intelligent and beautiful, skilled in composing poetry and prose. Therefore, they built a pavilion behind their residence to house them, naming it the Lian Fang Pavilion. Coincidentally, the monk Xuechuang from Chenting Temple, who was adept at painting orchids and lilies with ink and wash, was invited to paint the walls. The walls were coated with powder, and he painted upon them, creating an atmosphere that felt as if one had entered a room bathed in the warmth of spring. The two daughters continuously composed poetry day and night within the pavilion, producing hundreds of poems, which were titled *Lian Fang Ji*. Enthusiasts often circulated and recited these poems. At that time, Yang Tiayan of Kuaiji composed the *Zhuzhi Qu* of West Lake, and over a hundred families responded with their own compositions, which were engraved on woodblocks and published in bookshops. The two daughters, upon seeing this, smiled and said, "If West Lake has a *Zhuzhi Qu*, does Eastern Wu lack one?" Thus, they imitated its style and composed ten chapters of the *Zhuzhi Qu* of Suzhou, which read:

The moon above Gusu Terrace is round and full, while the water below it flows gently and softly. The moon sets in the west at times, but when will the water flowing east return? In the Palace of Gua Wa, deer roam freely, while Xi Shi has departed on her boat to the Five Lakes. Where does the fragrant soul and delicate bones of Xi Shi return to? It is not as good as Zhen Niang being buried at Tiger Hill. On the hills of Tiger Hill, there are many layers of pagodas, and at night, the Buddhist lamps can be seen clearly. I will visit the temple with friends to burn incense, and I will offer my jewelry to the mountain monks. There are ships from the eastern port, and the water is shrouded in mist as the moon sets and the crows cry. The early bell ringing at Hanshan Temple, the fishing lights and river maple trees disturb the traveler's sleep. The golden tangerines from Dongting are three inches long, while the silver fish from Lize are one foot long. The local delicacies are known by few, and the fine food cannot be brought to the imperial court. The reed shoots sprout and the lilac flowers bloom, but the pufferfish and stonefish do not come. The morning air is filled with the smell of fish, and the man returns from the coast with fresh seafood. The willow trees change color from green to yellow, and the seasons pass. I am like the willow silk, easily withered, while the man is like the willow fluff, too carefree and reckless. The jade green birds fly together without being called, while the mandarin ducks sleep together, never alone. I hate the water at the Baodai Bridge, which flows into both the Wujiang River and Taihu Lake. The woman with the phoenix hairdo green as clouds, and the eight-character tooth comb white as silver. She leans against the red door and looks up, and there are many heartbroken people coming and going. The hundred-foot tall building leans against the blue sky, and the curved railings and painted screens are connected. We have our own music of Gusu Terrace, and we do not go to West Lake to sing the song of picking lotus flowers.

His works are all equally excellent, and his talent can be known. Iron Cliff saw his manuscript and wrote two poems afterward: The Jiang of Brocade is only known for Xue Tao's letters, but in Wu Prefecture, the *Lan Hui* chapter is now passed down. The elegance and brilliance of his writing are known to have their source, like pearls and jade shining at the feast. His brothers, both talented, are equally famous, and their beauty and grace are not inferior to that of the precious jade. How wonderful to take the spring breeze from the tip of his pen and set it to the music of a jade zither. Thus, his fame spread far and wide, and all believed that Ban Jieyu and Cai Wenji had returned, surpassing Li Qingzhao and Zhu Shuzhen and others. The lower part of his building overlooked the official river, and boats passed by. There was a young man named Zheng Sheng from Kunshan, also a member of a prominent family, whose father had a close relationship with Xue, and thus sent Zheng to trade in the area. Upon arrival, he moored his boat below the building and relied on Xue as his host. Xue treated him as a close family member due to his father's relationship, and they came and went together without any barriers. Zheng, being young, had a gentle and refined temperament. In the summer months, he bathed at the bow of his boat, and the two women, seeing him through the window, threw down a pair of lychees. Although Zheng understood their intention, he still looked up at the towering and lofty building, which seemed to float in the sky, and unless he had wings, he could not reach it. As the night grew deeper and the moon fell into the river, all sounds ceased, and he stood on the ship's rail, as if waiting for something. Suddenly, he heard a sound from the window, and in the blink of an eye, the two women lowered a bamboo basket on a swing rope, which fell in front of him. Zheng then climbed into it and ascended. When he met them, he was so overjoyed that he could not speak, and they took him to bed, fully expressing their affection. The eldest daughter spontaneously composed a poem to give to him:

On a stone terrace with carved railings, two flowers bloom, and we meet just as they are about to open. Their graceful appearance is not yet accustomed to the winds and rains of life, so I instruct the East Lord to take good care of them. The younger daughter also recites: The smoke from the incense fades, and the candlelight dims; the screen at the foot of the bed gently shakes, holding the silk curtains in place. Her grace is like a fish swimming in water, moving fluidly from east to west. At dawn, they descend together again, and so they meet every night without fail. The two daughters have many more poems, but not all can be remembered. Feeling ashamed for not being able to respond with a poem, one evening, he sees a fine piece of paper from the Yanshan River area on the table and writes a poem on it, saying:

Mistakenly entering the top of the Penglai Mountain, lotus and peony flowers bloom on both sides. This body could be like a butterfly that steals fragrance, playing among the flowers how many times a day. The two girls received the poem, greatly delighted, and hid it in their chests. Soon after, they went to bed, and the man again requested their verses. The eldest girl immediately sang: On the intertwined branches, a double-flowered bloom, a pearl of inestimable value and jade without flaws. The younger girl continued: Fortunately, we have met in harmony like Xianghua, but it is hard to visit each other in the same spontaneous spirit as Xie Xuan and his friend. The eldest girl then added: Silk stockings walking on dust, the soul feels light and carefree; a jade hairpin falls onto the pillow. The younger girl concluded: Some day, revealing the news of spring, we will not regret tonight's momentary mistake. Thus, they completed a regulated poem. Another night, after midnight, the man suddenly felt melancholy and said, "I am a traveler, relying on your door; today's events, your parents may not know. Once the facts become known, affection and feelings may be interrupted; the mirror of Lechang may be divided forever; the sword of Yanping, who knows when it will be reunited again." He choked and wept. The two girls said, "We are aware of our own shortcomings. Living long in the inner chambers, we have a rough understanding of the classics and histories. We are not ignorant of the ugliness of drilling through walls or the beauty of keeping treasures. However, the autumn moon and spring flowers often make us lament the wasted time, our emotions and natures unstable. In the past, we secretly peered at the wall of Song Yu, offering our own jade. Grateful for your kindness, you have graciously accepted us, though the six rites have not been performed, I believe our words are already agreed upon."

She was just about to share joy on the bed, forever serving the clothing and headgear, how could she suddenly say this, causing her own doubts and obstacles? Vizier, Vizier, I, though a woman, have carefully considered it! One day, the secret matter will become known, and the father will rebuke me. If you follow my request, I will finally serve the broom and dustpan in your family; if not achieving what is planned, seek me in the netherworld, and I will never step into another door again. One day, she climbed the tower and found the man's poem in the chest, greatly startled. However, the matter has come to this, and there is nothing to be done. Considering the man is also a handsome youth, and our families are also well-matched, she then wrote a letter to the man's father, expressing her intention. The man's father agreed to her request. Still, he ordered the matchmaker to arrange the union of the two families, inquiring about the name and offering gifts, arranging for him to marry into the family. At that time, the man was twenty-two years old, the eldest daughter was twenty, and the youngest daughter was eighteen. Many people in Wu knew about it, or it was recorded as a matter of record.

*Volume Two of *New Tales of the Lamp*** **The Record of Linghu's Ghostly Dream* Linghu Zuan was a man of integrity and uprightness, born without belief in gods and spirits, and he was arrogant and self-satisfied. Whenever someone spoke of the transformations and retributions of ghosts and spirits in the underworld, he would always vehemently refute them. Near his residence lived an elderly man named Wu, who was extremely wealthy, greedy, and ceaselessly sought more, daring to commit unjust acts, and his wickedness was notorious. One night, he fell ill and died; three days later, he revived. When people asked him why, he said, "After my death, my family widely performed Buddhist rituals and burned many paper money offerings. The officials of the underworld were pleased with this, and therefore, I was spared." Upon hearing this, Linghu was especially resentful and said, "At first, I thought that in this world, corrupt officials and bureaucrats would bend the law for money, the rich would bribe their way to success, and the poor would be punished for lack of wealth. How could I have expected that the underworld is even worse!" He then composed a poem: A single sheet of paper money can bring the dead back to life, Public and private matters can be resolved through bribery everywhere! The gods and spirits have virtue and open the path to life, But the sun and moon do not shine on the overturned pot.

How can the poor ever receive the power of the Buddha? The wealthy easily receive the grace of heaven. If only I had known that good and evil are not rewarded, I would have amassed gold to leave for my descendants! The poem was completed, and he recited it aloud several times. That night, sitting alone with four candles burning, suddenly two spirit messengers appeared before him, their appearances fierce and sinister. They approached him and said, "The underworld has summoned you." Zuan was greatly startled and was about to try to refuse or turn away, but one seized his robe and another pulled his sash, forcing him out the door. His feet did not touch the ground, and in an instant they had arrived. He saw a grand official hall, resembling the administrative offices of the earthly world. The two messengers led Zuan inside and looked up to see a king wearing a crown seated at a table. The messengers seized Zuan and made him kneel at the base of the steps. They then ascended the hall and announced, "We have arrived as commanded to pursue Linghu Zuan." Immediately, the king spoke in a harsh voice: "Having studied the Confucian classics, yet failing to examine yourself, you dare to speak recklessly and slander my officials! You should be sent to the Tongue-Pulling Hell." Soon, several ghost officials appeared and dragged him away. Zuan was greatly terrified and tried to hold onto the railings, but could not resist. Suddenly, the railing broke, and he cried out, "Linghu Zuan is a scholar of the human world, and I am innocent yet being punished! May Heaven above know this, and grant me clear judgment!" At that moment, a man in a green robe holding a tablet appeared in the hall, known as the Clarifier of Law, who addressed the king, saying, "This man is known for his accusations. To hastily condemn him will surely lead to resistance. It would be better to have him confess his crimes and clearly establish his guilt, and then he will have no defense." The king said, "Good!" Then a clerk appeared, holding paper and brush and placing them before Zuan, compelling him to write a confession. Zuan firmly declared his innocence, yet did not know what to write.

Suddenly, a voice from the hall declared: "Your words are not guilty, but what does this mean: 'With a small amount of money, one can easily gain access, whether in public or private matters'? Who said this?" The author suddenly realized the truth and immediately wrote a detailed response: "I submit that in the primordial chaos of the two fundamental forces, the shape of heaven and earth was initially divided; the three fundamental elements—Heaven, Earth, and Humanity—are not counted among the numbers of ghosts and gods. Descending from the middle ages, many practices began to emerge. Paper money and silk were burned to communicate with spirits, and scriptures were recited to flatter the Buddha. Thus, famous mountains and large lakes are all inhabited by spirits; ancient temples and shrines also have many masters. This is because all beings are dim-sighted, and all kinds are stubborn and ignorant. Some continue evil without repentance, and some commit violence and act at will. They use strength to bully the weak and rely on wealth to deceive the poor. They are disobedient to superiors and parents and unfriendly to relatives and clan members. They are greedy for wealth and contrary to justice, seeing profit and forgetting gratitude."

The heavens are high and their nine layers unknown, the underworld is deep with its ten courts arrayed, and there are the instruments of punishment such as the guillotine, fire, mortar, and mill, along with the detailed system of karma and retribution. This encourages the virtuous to become even more diligent and punishes the wicked so they know to refrain from evil, which can be said to be the law's utmost meticulousness and the way's utmost fairness. However, the strict enforcement of laws is both forward-looking and backward-looking, while human intelligence, though perceptive, often overlooks the bigger picture. The poor are imprisoned and suffer, while those who enter the palace can avoid punishment by reciting sutras. The law only captures the wary bird, often missing the larger fish. The rules of reward and punishment should not be like this. As for the author, a humble and poor scholar through three lifetimes, one who has had a difficult and sorrowful life, with futile and unsuccessful efforts, occasionally speaking out against injustice and thus incurring criticism, regretting not being able to change the past and feeling ashamed of pleading for mercy. Now, being held accountable and forced to admit guilt.

批龙鳞，探尤颔，岂敢求生；料虎头，编虎须，固知受祸。言止此矣，伏乞鉴之！王览毕，批曰：“令狐譔持论颇正，难以罪加，秉志不回，非可威屈。今观所陈，实为有理，可特放还，以彰遗直。 ”仍命复追乌老，置之于狱。复遣二使送譔还家。譔恳二使曰：“仆在人间，以儒为业，虽闻地狱之事，不以为然，今既到此，可一观否？ ”二使曰：“欲观亦不难，但禀知刑曹录事耳。 ”即引譔循西廊而行，别至一厅，文簿山积，录事中坐，二使以譔入白，录事以朱笔批一帖付之，其文若篆籒不可识。譔出府门，投北行里余，见铁城巍巍，黑雾涨天，守卫者甚众，皆牛头曳面，青体绀发，各执戈戟之属，或坐或立于门左右。二使以批帖示之，即放之入，见罪人无数，被剥皮刺血，剔心剜目。叫呼怨痛，宛转其间，楚毒之声动地。至一处，见铜柱二，缚男女二人于上，有夜叉以刃剖其胸，肠胃流出，以沸汤沃之，名为洗涤。譔问其故。 To criticize the emperor, to dare to challenge powerful forces, how could one dare to seek life? To predict the intentions of a dangerous person, to handle a dangerous situation with extreme care, one firmly knows that calamity will befall. The words end here, I humbly request your judgment! The king, after reading, wrote: "Lingfu Zhan holds a rather correct view, it is difficult to add to the charges, he holds steadfastly to his purpose without turning back, cannot be coerced by force. Now observing what is presented, truly has reason, can be specially released, to highlight the remaining integrity." He still ordered to pursue Ula, placing him in prison. He then sent two envoys to send Zhan back home. Zhan pleaded with the two envoys, saying: "I have been in the world, taking Confucianism as my profession, though I have heard of the matters of hell, I did not believe it, now that I have arrived here, can I take a look?" The two envoys said: "To take a look is also not difficult, but you must inform the Recorder of the Criminal Department." They immediately led Zhan along the west corridor and to a different hall, where documents piled up like mountains, the Recorder sat in the middle, the two envoys entered to report, the Recorder wrote a note with a red pen and gave it to him, the text was like ancient script and was not recognizable. Zhan left the government gate, traveled north for several miles, saw a towering iron city, black mist filling the sky, many guards, all with ox heads dragging their faces, green bodies and indigo hair, each holding weapons such as spears and halberds, some sitting or standing on either side of the door. The two envoys showed him the note, immediately allowing him to enter, where he saw countless criminals, having their skin peeled and blood drawn, their hearts removed and eyes gouged out. They cried out in pain and resentment, twisting and turning among them, the sounds of torture shook the earth. At one place, he saw two copper pillars, two men and women bound to them, a yaochi (demon) used a blade to cut open their chests, their intestines spilled out, poured boiling water over them, named it cleansing. Zhan asked the reason.

The person said, "This man was a doctor in the world. Because he treated the wife's husband, he then had an affair with the wife. Later, the husband died of illness, although the two did not directly cause his death, considering the circumstances and determining the crime, it is the same as murder, hence he received this punishment." He then went to another place and saw monks and nuns stripped naked, with various ghosts covering them with cow and horse skins, transforming them into animal forms. Those who hesitated to accept this transformation were immediately struck with iron whips, causing them to bleed profusely. The person asked the reason for this. They replied, "These people in the world, without farming, ate and without weaving, wore clothes, yet they did not abide by the precepts, indulging in lust and eating meat, so they were transformed into different kinds of beings, to repay people with effort." Finally, he arrived at a place marked with a sign that read, "The Gate of Betraying the Nation." He saw several dozen people sitting on iron beds, bound with shackles, and their necks were pressed down by large stone collars.

Two envoys pointed to a person and told Zuan, "This is Qin Hui of the Song Dynasty. He plotted to harm loyal ministers and misled his sovereign, therefore he suffered a severe punishment. The others are also officials from past dynasties who betrayed their country. Whenever a new dynasty is established, they are driven out and made to suffer as venomous snakes devour their flesh and hungry eagles peck at their marrow, until their flesh and bones are completely devoured. Then they are sprinkled with divine water and blown by the wind of karma, and they return to their original forms. These individuals, even after countless eons, cannot be reborn into the world." Zuan finished looking and asked to return. The two envoys escorted him home. Zuan turned and said, "Thank you for seeing me off, but I have no way to repay you." The envoys laughed and said, "We do not expect repayment, but please do not write more poems that will burden us." Zuan also laughed. He yawned and stretched, then woke up, realizing it was a dream. By dawn, he went to the home of Old Owl and asked about it, and it was said that he passed away at the third watch of that night.

**Record of Visiting Recluses at Tiantai** A man from Taizhou named Xu Yi had only a basic knowledge of books and history. On the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, he entered Wutai Mountain to gather herbs. A few others had gone with him, but fearing the dangers ahead, they turned back halfway. Only Xu Yi was captivated by the clear mountains and beautiful waters, the dense shade of the trees, and proceeded without knowing when to stop. He recited a poem by Sun Xinggong and praised its excellence, saying: “Chicheng rises with a thunderous surge and establishes its mark; waterfalls and springs flow and define the path.” Indeed, these words were not empty. Going another few li ahead, the sun slanted over the hilltop, birds flew into the forest, and there was nowhere to go forward, and it was too late to return. While hesitating, he suddenly saw a large gourd floating out from the stream. Delighted, he said, “Could there be a resident here? Otherwise, it must be a Daoist temple or a Buddhist monastery.” He then followed the stream, for a little over a li, until he reached a narrow pass. There, a huge stone formed the entrance. He stepped inside for several dozen paces.

Then suddenly, the place appeared spacious and open, with about forty or fifty households, the residents dressed in ancient and simple attire, their demeanor sincere and thick. The fields were stony, the houses thatched, the doors made of bamboo, and the gates woven with thorns. Dogs barked and roosters crowed, and mulberry trees and hemp plants interwove, creating a scene that was truly a village. Seeing Yi Lei, they were startled and asked, "Who are you, and how did you enter our territory?" Yi Lei explained that he had entered the mountain to gather herbs and lost his way here. They looked at each other without speaking, showing no intention of welcoming him. Only one old man, dressed like a scholar, carried a staff and approached, introducing himself as Tao Shang She from the Imperial College.
