He bowed to Yi Lei and said, "The mountains and marshes are deep and dangerous, where wolves howl and demons roam. The day is already late. If you firmly reject me, it would be like seeing someone drowning and not helping." He then invited Yi Lei to return to his room. After sitting down, Yi Lei stood up and asked, "I was born here, grew up here, and have been here for a long time. I have never heard of this village. I dare to ask." Tao Shang She frowned and replied, "Those who avoid the world and those who flee from trouble. If you recount past events, it will only increase sadness." Yi Lei insisted on knowing the reason. The old man began, "I have been living here since the Song Dynasty." Yi Lei was greatly surprised.

The guest then elaborately stated: "I was born in the year Ding You of the Jiaxi era during the reign of Emperor Lizong. After growing up, I enrolled in the Imperial Academy, residing in the Ratefuli Hall and being renowned for my lectures on the *I Ching* (Book of Changes). During the reign of Emperor Duozong, I twice passed the hall examination and was once recommended to the provincial examination. I intended to establish myself and gain fame in the world, but unfortunately, Emperor Duozong passed away, and the Empress Dowager assumed regency. The northern army crossed the Yangtze River, and the situation underwent great changes. The successor emperor changed the era name to Deyou, and I fled with my family to this place. The other people here also fled at the same time. After many years, we settled down here. We grew grain and gathered firewood, dug wells for water, and built houses for shelter. Seasons changed, and days passed. We only saw flowers bloom in spring and leaves fall in autumn, and we did not know what dynasty or year it was."

The guest said, "The present emperor is wise, martial, and divinely inspired, continuing the legacy and initiating a new era, unifying the Chinese lands, and establishing the state of Great Ming. The current year is the seventh year of the Hongwu era, during the year of the sexagenary cycle known as 'Yuan Feng She Ti Ge.'" The host replied, "Ah, I only know about the Song dynasty, not the Yuan, and how would I know that today is the era of Great Ming! I wish the guest could briefly recount the rise and fall of the three dynasties so that I may hear it." The guest then said, "In the year of Ding Si during the Song dynasty, the Yuan forces invaded Lin'an, and the imperial family and court were relocated to the north. That year, Guang Wang established a new court on the sea, changing the era name to Jing Yan. Soon after, he died, and was posthumously titled as the Duan Emperor. Yi Wang succeeded him, but was forced by the Yuan forces to commit suicide, leading to the demise of the Song dynasty, which occurred in the year of Wu Yin during the Yuan dynasty. The Yuan then unified the north and south, and by the year of Ding Wei during the Yuan dynasty, it had passed through half of a sexagenary cycle and eventually perished. Now, the Great Ming dynasty has been established, and it is the seventh year of the Hongwu era. It has been nearly a century since the year of Ding Si in the Song dynasty." The host, upon hearing this, could not help but shed tears. As the night grew quiet and the mountains became empty, the sounds around them grew still. The guest stayed in the host's room, where the bed was made of earth and the pillow of stone, yet it was very neat. However, the guest felt mentally clear and physically cool, unable to fall asleep. The next day, the host killed a chicken and prepared millet, serving the guest wine in a simple earthenware vessel. The host composed a poem called "Gold Thread Lyrics," singing to accompany the drinking.

When one awakens from a dream, the yellow millet is cooked. Strange is the world, where a different tune is played and a new chessboard is turned over. A scholar with white hair endures longer. Tilling the green fields and feeding the yellow calves, one finds contentment in the countryside. The bustling city transforms into a desolate landscape. Who will be honored and who will be disgraced? The white-haired scholar endures longer. In the forest, one can laugh and rest in the countryside. While the chancellor was boasting about peace and prosperity, misleading the emperor's ears, once the enemy's horses drink from the river, the family and country are overthrown. How could the chancellor long enjoy such wealth and status? Thus, he choked himself to death. Xie Tang, the uncle of the empress dowager, was incredibly wealthy. He once held a night banquet for guests, setting up crystal curtains and burning sandalwood incense. Using a jade plate a foot in diameter, he placed four large pearls, their light illuminating the room, making no need for lamps or candles. Entertainers recited auspicious words, presenting a golden seven-treasure wine jar weighing several dozen pounds, which he immediately bestowed upon them without hesitation. After the empress dowager took the throne, she dreamed that the sky was tilting to the southeast. One person was holding it up, his strength barely sufficient, falling and rising three times. Later, one day it fell to the ground, and beside it, a person held it up and ran. Upon waking, she searched throughout the court and found two people who looked exactly like them: the one holding up the sky was Wen Tianxiang, and the one holding up the sun was Lu Shoufu. She then promoted them without regard to seniority. When Jiang Wanli left the country, thousands of citizens followed him out of the city, clinging to the carriage and reluctant to let go. After the city gates were closed, many stayed overnight in the fields. When Jia Sidao was dispatched, he wore silver armor and a pearl-studded saddle. He had two thousand-mile horses, one carrying the seal of the governor's office, and the other carrying the imperial edict and military reward chart, covered with yellow silk. The citizens stopped their businesses to watch. The grandeur of his departure was unprecedented. He further commented on the officials of that time: "Chen Yizhong was thoughtful but indecisive, Jia Xuanweng was principled but ineffective, Zhang Shijie was brave but hesitant, and Li Tingzhi was intelligent but unwise. The best among them was Wen Tianxiang." Such were his words, numbering in the hundreds, all clear and audible.

That night, Yi stayed again. The next morning, he announced his departure, and the host composed a poem in the style of "Gufeng" (ancient ballad) to see him off, saying: The move southward during Jianyan was marked by many upheavals, with the emperor escaping on a mud horse to govern the yellow palace. All the old possessions were handed over to others, and Jiangnan became a separate realm. Pitifully, two young maids served wine, and a myriad of sorrows and regrets, who can know them? The cold moonlight shone over the city of Wuguo, and the north wind blew over the Yellow Dragon Pass. The plan was hatched in the east chamber to achieve peace, and E Wang was executed while Qi Wang grew old. Dong Si Xiang was not seen in the wine, and one must look for Song Wu Sao by the lake. For generations, the throne was passed internally, and war was no longer spoken of, and for over eighty years, it was called a time of peace. The emperor's death left the bow and sword far away, and Jia Xiang led the army, with the sound of drums startling the enemy. Taking the family to hide in the empty valley, looking west toward the main gate and crying with bowed head, destroying the chariot and killing the horse to sever all traces, digging wells and farming to barely make ends meet. Marrying within the southern and northern neighbors, the old customs resembling those of Zhu Chen Village, not serving in the city as a laborer or paying taxes, only growing children and grandchildren under the roof. I am glad you came to visit despite the dangers, often asking about the old and supporting the nine-jointed staff. The times have changed, and events have shifted too quickly, the things remain the same, but the people have changed, causing even more sorrow. I am grateful that you stayed with me for a while, using wild vegetables and mountain delicacies as offerings.

The Chickens at Home Are Fat, Why Buy More? The Wine by the Bed Is Ready, No Need for More. When you come to the human world, please convey my message: Now that peace and prosperity have been achieved, there is a place of joy and peace. We do not need to doubt each other when we meet; we are neither gods nor ghosts. So I sent Yi out at the crossroads, waving goodbye and parting. Yi placed a bamboo stick every fifty steps along the way to mark it. After returning home after several days, he prepared wine and spirits, carried food and dishes, and led the household servants to visit the place. However, the mountains were too steep and the terrain too complex to find again, with lush grass and tall trees, no trace at all. Walking along the paths of woodcutters and shepherds, he only heard the birds in the valley crying mournfully and the apes on the mountain wailing sadly, and finally returned with a sense of melancholy. Yi remembered that the person at the inn said they were born in the year Ding You of Jiaxi, and now they are one hundred and forty years old, but their appearance has not aged, their speech and actions are refined and elegant, as if only fifty or sixty years old, could they be a person of Dao? Record of Teng Mu's Drunken Wandering in the Juyi Garden In the early years of the Yanyou era, Teng Mu from Yongjia was twenty-six years old, with a beautiful demeanor and temperament, skilled in poetry, and highly praised by many. He had always heard of the beauty of the mountains and rivers of Lin'an and thought to visit once. In the year of Jia Yin, the imperial examination was held, and he then went to take the exam. Upon arrival, he temporarily resided outside the Yongjin Gate, going every day to the two mountains north and south and various temples by the lake, such as Lingyin, Tiantu, Jingci, Gemstone, Yuping, Hupao, Tianlong, and Lingjiu. The cave of Shiwu, the pavilion of Lengquan, deep valleys and dense forests, steep cliffs and sheer rocks, his footsteps almost covered everything. On the full moon of July, he admired lotus flowers at Quyuan and so stayed by the lake, anchoring under Leifeng Pagoda.

That night, the moonlight was like daylight, and the fragrance of lotus flowers filled the air. Occasionally, one heard the sound of large fish leaping in the waves, and birds that had settled flew and called by the shore. The protagonist, already deeply drunk, lay in bed but could not sleep. He unfastened his robe and rose, walking around the embankment and looking around. He walked to the Juying Garden and entered at his leisure. At that time, forty years had passed since the fall of the Song Dynasty, and the pavilions and halls in the garden, such as the Hui Fang Hall, Qing Hui Pavilion, and Cui Guang Pavilion, had all fallen into disrepair. Only the Western Pavilion of the Jade Spring remained standing. The protagonist reached the pavilion, leaned against the railing, and rested for a moment. Suddenly, he saw a beautiful woman leading the way, followed by a maid, entering from outside. Her hair was like wind and her hairpins like mist, and she was graceful and full of charm, looking like a fairy. The protagonist held his breath and watched her actions. The woman said, "The lakes and mountains remain unchanged, and the scenery is no different, but time has changed and the world has transformed, making one feel the sorrow of the *Shu Li*!" She walked to the north of the garden, by the Taihu stone, and then recited a poem: The gardens by the lake are beautiful, and returning reminds one of past travels. Singing songs in the style of *Yushu*, dancing in the style of *Liangzhou*. The narrow path is lined with flowers greeting the carriage, the deep pool has willows brushing the boat. The former people have all died, who can talk about elegance? The protagonist, a free-spirited man, was immediately captivated by her appearance at first sight. Upon hearing her poem, he felt an irresistible urge to write and, without restraint, continued to recite his own poem at the pavilion:

The garden by the lake is beautiful, and I have met a person of unparalleled beauty. Chang'e has left her palace on the moon, and Zhinv has descended from the Milky Way. I do not understand her inner feelings, and I feel as if I am in a dream. I wish to play the music of Zou Yan, bringing spring to a remote valley. After reciting, I hurriedly went out to meet her. The beautiful lady was not surprised but calmly said, "I knew you were here, and I came specifically to visit you." The man asked her name, and she replied, "I have been away from the human world for a long time. If I were to tell my story, I fear it might startle you." Upon hearing this, the man realized she was a ghost but felt no fear. He insisted on knowing more, and she said, "My name is Wei Fanghua. I was a palace lady during the reign of Emperor Lizong of the Song Dynasty. I died at the age of twenty-three and was buried beside this garden. Tonight, I went to visit Princess Jia at the Yanyu Palace. I was invited to stay for a long time and didn't realize I was late, causing you to wait for so long." She then ordered her maid, "Qiao Qiao, go to the house and bring out the mats, wine, and fruits. The moon is so beautiful tonight, and you are here, so we cannot waste this time. Let us enjoy the moon here." Qiao Qiao obeyed and went to fetch them. Soon, she brought a purple rug, set up a white jade flower carver, and placed green glass cups. The wine was fragrant and unique, not found in the world. They laughed and recited poetry together, with her words being clear and elegant. She then ordered Qiao Qiao to sing to accompany the wine. Qiao Qiao requested to sing a song by Liu Qi卿's "Looking at the Sea Tide," but the lady said, "It is not appropriate to sing old songs for a new person." She then composed a new piece of "Mu Lan Hua Man" on the spot and ordered Qiao Qiao to sing it.

Recording the old affairs of the previous dynasty, once here, I met the immortals. Facing the moon, stepping on the cloud-strewn stairs, I again carried the green sleeves, coming to pick up the flower ornaments. Prosperity always follows the flowing water; I lament a spring dream that is hard to fulfill. In the abandoned harbor, the lotus drops dew; on the broken dike, willows hang in smoke. The two peaks in the north and south remain unchanged; the imperial road is covered with lush grass. Hating the guesthouses and imperial palaces, the smoke dissipates from the phoenix canopy, the waves submerge the dragon boat. In the past, the silver screens and golden houses, facing the lamp with no flame, the night feels like a year. The setting sun on the hillside, with cattle and sheep; the west wind at the edge of the forest, with swallows and sparrows. When the song ended, the beautiful woman silently shed tears. I comforted her with words, still gently teasing her to see her intentions. She immediately rose and thanked me, saying, "A person who has passed away, long turned to dust, if I could serve you by tending to your daily needs, I would be immortal even in death. And your verses just now, they have already promised me. I wish you could blow the law of Zou Zi, and release the spring from the depths of the valley." I replied, "The poetry I composed earlier was spoken offhand, truly without any intention, how could I have expected it to become an omen." After a long while, the moon hid behind the western wall, the river tilted eastward toward the mountain, and I immediately ordered the removal of the table. The woman said, "My humble abode is remote and shabby, not a place for you to stay; only this western pavilion would be suitable." So we took each other's hands and entered, taking a temporary rest beneath the pavilion. The meeting that took place was just like in the human world. As dawn approached, we parted ways with tears.

During the day, he visited the side of the garden and indeed found the tomb of Wei Fanghua, a palace woman from the Song dynasty. To the left of the tomb was a small mound, which was the "翘翘瘗." The protagonist was moved and reflected for a long time. By evening, he again went to the western pavilion, and the beauty had already arrived. She greeted him and said, "I was moved by your visit, but I could only foresee the night, not the day, so I dared not meet you. After several days, we will meet continuously." From then on, they met every night. After a few weeks, they also met during the day. The protagonist then brought her back to his residence and settled her there. Soon after, the protagonist failed the examination and returned home, and the beauty wished to accompany him. The protagonist asked, "Why is '翘翘' not accompanying us?" She replied, "I am serving the gentleman, and the old house is empty, so I left '翘翘' to watch over it." The protagonist and the beauty returned to his hometown together, and when he met his relatives, he told them, "I married into a good family in Hangzhou." They believed and were pleased with her. Neighbors and those around her were all pleased with her. She was diligent in managing the household and pure in self-discipline. Though she never went out lightly beyond the main gate, everyone congratulated the protagonist on gaining an excellent wife.

Three years had passed, and it was the early autumn of the Ding Si year. The scholar once again prepared his belongings and set out for the provincial examination in Zhejiang. After several days of travel, the beauty requested of the scholar, saying, "Lin'an is my hometown. I have been with you here for three autumns. Now, I wish to travel with you, to look back fondly." The scholar agreed, and they rented a boat and traveled together, directly to Qiantang, where they rented a house to live in. The next day, it happened to be the full moon night of the seventh month. The beauty said to the scholar, "Three years ago, we met on this very night. Now, it is the same time, and I wish to go with you to Juyi Garden, to continue our old tour, is it possible?" The scholar agreed as said, and they carried wine and went.

By evening, the moon rose above the eastern wall, the lotus bloomed in the southern shore, dewy willows and misty bamboo groves swayed gently against the embankments, the scene as if it were the same as of old. When he reached the garden gate, a figure stood eagerly at the path’s head and bowed respectfully, saying, “Lady, you have accompanied Lord Sir on wanderings through the city for three full years, having already reached the height of earthly joy. Do you not remember your old home?” The three entered the garden and sat together in the western pavilion. Suddenly, the beauty wept and told the man, “I am grateful that you did not cast me aside, and that I have served in your household. Yet our deep joy has not yet been fulfilled, and now we must part forever.” The man asked, “Why?” She replied, “I am by nature a being of the spirit world, long dwelling in the realm of light and brightness. It is most unsuitable for me to remain here. I dared to defy the laws of the cosmos merely to be with you. But now our karmic bond has come to an end, and I must take my leave.” The man was shocked and asked, “Then when will this be?” She answered, “Only tonight.” The man was filled with sorrow and could not bear the parting. The beauty said, “It is not that I do not wish to remain with you until the end, to serve you in joy forever. But fate is limited, and we cannot defy it. If I linger any longer, I shall suffer punishment. It will not only harm me, but also bring you harm. Have you not heard of the tale of Yue Nü?” The man’s mind slightly understood, yet he was still filled with sorrow and grief, and could not sleep all night. When the temple bell rang in the mountain and the rooster crowed in the village by the water, she quickly rose to take her leave of him, removing the jade ring from her finger and fastening it to his belt. She said, “When you see this in the future, do not forget our old affection.” Then she parted from him, yet still turned back repeatedly, and only after a long while did she finally disappear. The man wept bitterly and returned home.

The next day, food and wine were prepared, and paper money and paper offerings were burned at the grave. An essay was written to mourn and offer sacrifices, saying: May the spirit have been born beautiful and virtuous, standing out and excelling among others. It received extraordinary beauty from the immortals and sages, and gathered the finest qualities from heaven and earth. Brightly like the beauty of flowers, purely like the warmth of jade. If successful, to live in a golden house in heaven; if poor, to be buried in a lonely grave by the roadside. To rest under the pines and cypresses together, to face the running of foxes and rabbits. Fallen flowers and flowing water, broken rain and scattered clouds, there are many troubles in the Central Plains, the old country has no ruler. To feel the fleeting nature of time, to see the sun and moon racing. However, the spirit does not perish, the nature and knowledge remain forever. It is not necessary to rely on the strange techniques of the wizard, the spirit can return on its own. A jade box with a fan carried by a phoenix, a golden skirt with clusters of butterflies. The sound of the jade pendants is clear and cold, the fragrance of the orchids and sweet grass is pervasive. Just as we were about to enjoy happiness together until old age, how can we have been united and then separated! To walk in the shoes of the Luo River fairy, crossing the waves, to go to the wine cup of the Queen Mother of the West in the Jade Pool. To see nothing when approaching, to hear nothing when knocking. To lament that future meetings cannot be continued, to grieve that past events cannot be discussed. To lock the courtyard of willows in the spring breeze, to close the door of pear blossoms in the night rain. The affection has been severed, and the sky is vast and empty, grief and resentment have formed, and the clouds are dark and heavy. The voice and appearance are distant and unreachable, the heart and mind are confused and chaotic. To respectfully offer condolences with sorrow, perhaps this writing will touch someone. Alas, alas, may the spirit enjoy the offering.

From this point onward, they were completely severed. The man lived alone in a temporary lodging, feeling as if he had lost his spouse. The exam period was already approaching, and he had no heart to enter the academy; he returned home in a state of melancholy. When relatives and friends asked him about the reason, he began to fully recount it, and everyone expressed admiration or surprise. Afterward, he remained unmarried for life, entered Yandang Mountain to gather herbs, and never returned. **The Peony Lantern Record** During the time when the Fang family occupied Zhe Dong, every year during the Lantern Festival, in Mingzhou, lanterns were displayed for five nights, and all the city's men and women were allowed to view them. In the year 1350, there was a man named Qiao who lived at the foot of Zhenming Ridge. He had recently lost his wife and lived in a state of loneliness and boredom, no longer going out. He merely leaned against the door and stood there. On the 15th night, at the end of the third watch, the number of tourists gradually decreased. He saw a maidservant carrying a double-headed peony lantern in front, followed by a beautiful woman, about seventeen or eighteen years old, with a red skirt and green sleeves, graceful and slender, slowly and gracefully headed westward. He looked at her under the moonlight, and she had a beautiful face and youthful teeth, truly a national beauty. His soul and spirit were wandering, and he could not restrain himself. Then he followed her, sometimes leading her, sometimes following her.

After walking dozens of steps, the woman suddenly turned back and smiled slightly, saying, "We had no prearranged meeting, yet we met by the moonlight, which seems not by chance." The man immediately approached her and bowed, saying, "My home is very close, would a beautiful lady like you consider coming back?" The woman showed no reluctance and called out to her maid, "Jinpeng, let's carry a lamp and go together." Thus, Jinlian returned. The man and the woman walked hand in hand to his home, where they were extremely intimate and affectionate, feeling as if they were experiencing the legendary encounter between Emperor Xuanzong and Yang Guifei, not exaggerating it at all. The man asked her name and address, and she replied, "My surname is Fu, my courtesy name is Liqing, and my given name is Shufang. I am the daughter of a former state judge. My father has passed away, and the family affairs are in decline. I have no brothers, and there are few relatives. I am alone, so I live temporarily in the western part of the lake with Jinlian." The man kept her for the night. She was charming and graceful in demeanor, her words and tone were gentle and alluring. Under the low curtain, they were very intimate and loving. At dawn, she bid farewell and left, but she would come again at night.

For about half a month, things continued in this manner, and the old neighbor became suspicious. He poked a hole in the wall to look, and saw a white skeleton sitting with the living under the lamp, which greatly frightened him. The next morning, he questioned the young man, but the latter kept the matter secret and would not speak. The old neighbor said, “Ah! You are in great trouble! Humans are the purest yang, the most vigorous of all, while ghosts are foul and dark, the impure yin. Now you dwell with these spirits of the dark and obscure without knowing it, and share a dwelling with these impure things without realizing it. Once your true essence is exhausted, calamity will come upon you. It is a pity that in the prime of youth, you will become nothing but dust — is this not a most sorrowful fate?” The young man was then struck with fear and fully recounted the cause. The old neighbor said, “He said he resides in the western part of the lake. You should go there and search for him, and you will learn the truth.” The young man followed his advice and went directly to the western side of Moon Lake, moving back and forth along the long dike and under the high bridge, asking the local residents and inquiring of passersby — but all said they knew of no one.

The day was nearly over, and so he entered the Huaxin Temple to rest briefly. He walked through the eastern corridor and then turned around to walk through the western corridor. At the end of the corridor, he found a dark room where there was a coffin of a traveler. A white paper was inscribed on it, reading: "The coffin of Lady Liqing, daughter of the former Fu Zhou Judge." In front of the coffin hung a double-headed peony lamp, and under the lamp stood a figurine of a servant. On the back of the figurine were two characters that read "jinlian." Upon seeing this, the man's hair stood on end, and he shivered all over. He ran out of the temple without daring to look back. That night, he stayed at the neighbor's house, and his face showed visible fear and worry. The neighbor said, "The Taoist priest Wei from Xuanmiao Temple is a disciple of the former official and Taoist master Wang. His talismans are the best in the current era. You should go quickly and seek his help." The next morning, the man visited the temple. The Taoist priest saw him arrive and was surprised, saying, "The aura of evil is very strong. Why did you come here?" The man bowed before him and detailed the matter.

The master priest gave him two red talismans, instructing him to place one at the door and one on the bed, and warned him not to return to Huixin Temple. The young man received the talismans and returned home, placing them as instructed, and indeed, he did not come back. After more than a month, he went to Guxiu Bridge to visit a friend. He stayed to drink until he was drunk, completely forgetting the master priest's warning, and took the path directly to Huixin Temple on his way back. As he approached the temple gate, he saw a golden lotus flower bowing before him and said, "Lady, you have been waiting for a long time. Why have you been so cold-hearted?" She then took the young man with her into the west corridor, straight to the room. The woman was sitting there as if nothing had happened, and she scolded him, saying, "I have never met you before. We met by chance under the lamp, and I was moved by your intentions, so I devoted myself entirely to you, coming and going every day, treating you no differently. Why did you believe the words of the evil Taoist and suddenly doubt me? To be so unfaithful, I deeply resent you. Now that I have finally met you, how could I let you go?" She immediately took the young man's hand and led him to the coffin, which suddenly opened by itself. She embraced him and they both entered, and the coffin closed immediately. The young man died inside the coffin. The elderly neighbor was surprised that he had not returned and searched far and wide. When they reached the room in the temple where the coffin was placed, they saw the young man's sleeve slightly visible outside the coffin. They asked the temple monk to open the coffin, and it was found that the young man had been dead for a long time, lying with the woman's body inside, and the woman looked as if she were alive. The temple monk sighed and said, "This is the daughter of Fu Jun, the magistrate of Fenghua Prefecture. She died at the age of seventeen and was temporarily buried here. Her family moved north and there has been no news of them ever since, for twelve years now. Who would have thought that such strange things would happen!" They then buried the coffins and the young man outside the west gate.

After dark, on cloudy days and moonless nights, people often saw the man and the woman walking hand in hand, with a young servant girl carrying a lantern with two heads leading the way. Those who encountered them immediately fell seriously ill, suffering from fever and chills. To cure them, they offered good deeds and sacrifices with meat and wine, hoping for recovery. If not cured, the illness would be fatal. The residents were greatly afraid and all went to the Xuanmiao Temple to consult with Master Wei. Master Wei said, "My talismans can only prevent problems before they occur. Now the evil spirit has already manifested, and it is beyond my knowledge. I have heard of an Iron Hat Taoist who lives on the top of Four Bright Mountains. He investigates and punishes ghosts and spirits, and his magic is effective. You should go to seek him." The people then went to the mountain, climbing using vines and grass, leaping across streams, and ascending directly to the summit. Indeed, there was a thatched hut, and the Taoist was leaning on a table, watching a young boy tame a crane. The people knelt and bowed at the foot of the hut, telling him about the reason for their visit. The Taoist, a hermit living in the mountains, said, "I am about to die soon. How could I have any special skills! You have been misled."

The Daoist rejected them with great severity. The crowd said, "I originally did not know; it was taught by the mysterious Master Wei." He then said, "I have not come down from the mountain for sixty years. Youngster, you are talking too much, trouble me to go once." He immediately went down the mountain with his attendant, walking lightly and nimbly, and arrived at the western gate. He set up a square altar, sat cross-legged on the mat, wrote a talisman, and burned it. Suddenly, several officials of the talisman appeared, wearing yellow headbands and brocade robes, with golden armor and engraved spears, all over ten feet tall, standing firmly at the foot of the altar, bowing and asking for orders. Their appearance was very devout and solemn. The Daoist said, "There are evil spirits causing harm and disturbing the people here. You are not unaware of this, are you? They should quickly drive them here." Upon receiving the command, they went without delay, with shackles, they brought the woman, the man, and the golden lotus together. They whipped and beat them, causing blood to flow profusely. The Daoist scolded for a long time and ordered them to confess. The officials gave them paper and pens, and they each confessed several hundred words. Now recording their brief accounts here.

Qiao Sheng stated: I humbly reflect upon myself, a widower living alone, leaning on the door and standing alone, having violated the precept against lust and acting on many desires. Unable to act decisively like Sun Shuao who saw a two-headed snake, I instead indulged in the beauty of a nine-tailed fox like Zheng Ziqi. The matter can no longer be undone, what can be done now that we have been wronged! Fu Nu stated: I humbly reflect upon myself, a young woman who has left the world, alone during the day with no neighbors, my six spirits separated but my soul still intact. In the light of the lamp and moon, I met a beloved enemy after five hundred years; in the world and among the people, I became a tale of romance for millions. Lost and not returning, how can I escape blame! Jin Lian stated: I humbly reflect upon myself, a book with bones, a fetus formed from white paper, buried in a grave, who created me and used me? With facial features and organs, I am a miniature version of a human. Having a name and form, I lack the spiritual essence! Thus achieving my purpose, how dare I be an evil spirit! After the statements were completed, the officials took them to present. The Taoist priest judged with a large brush: I have heard that Yu the Great cast the tripod, and the forms of gods, demons, and spirits could not escape their true nature; Wen Qiao burned a rhinoceros horn, and the forms of dragons and palaces were revealed. Only the differences between the visible and invisible worlds, and the many forms of the strange. Those who encounter them are harmed, and those who suffer from them are damaged. Therefore, the ghost entering the gate caused the death of Duke Jing of Jin, and the demon pig crying in the wild caused the death of King Qi. Bringing disaster and becoming an evil spirit, causing calamity and committing sins. Thus, the gods and spirits in the nine heavens and ten earths set up officials to punish evil, making the demons and monsters unable to hide their evil, and the demons and ogres unable to act violently.

In this peaceful era and open time, they transform their forms and bodies, clinging to grass and trees, making sounds in the beams on rainy nights and early mornings when the moon sets, yet being unseen when observed. They live in a sycophantic and fawning manner, being as cruel and greedy as bulls and wolves, moving as swiftly as a gust of wind and burning as fiercely as a wildfire. The son of the Qiao family still does not understand when alive, how much less when dead? The daughter of the Fu family still desires lust even in death, how much more so when alive! How much more so, then, for the bizarre and fabricated nature of the "Golden Lotus," using false objects and deceit. Deceiving the world and misleading the people, violating laws and regulations. Foxes are wanton and quails are unprincipled. Evil deeds have reached their full measure, and no mercy will be shown. Traps are filled from now on, and confusion is opened from this point. The double lamps are burned, and they are sent to the underworld. The judgment is ready, and the executor shall carry it out swiftly as by law. Immediately, three people are seen weeping and hesitating, being driven away by officials. The Taoist waves his sleeves and enters the mountains. The next day, the crowd goes to thank him, but he is no longer visible, only a thatched hut remains. Hasten to the Xuanmiao Temple to consult with Master Wei for examination, but he is ill and mute, unable to speak.

A Strange Encounter at Wei塘 During the reign of Emperor Shun of the Yuan Dynasty, there was a young man named Wang, a member of the scholarly class, living in Jinling. He had a face as clear and cold as jade, and his gaze was calm and deep like autumn water. His appearance was exceptionally beautiful, and everyone praised him as the extraordinary and handsome young man from the Wang family. At the age of twenty, he was still unmarried. He owned land in Songjiang and went there to collect the autumn rent. On his return journey, he passed through Wei塘 and saw a wine shop. A green flag was hung outside the eaves; the red pillars and curved railings were picturesque, and the tall willows and ancient locust trees had their yellow leaves falling. There were ten or so lotus flowers, some deep and some light in color, with red petals and green water, reflecting each other above and below. A group of white geese swam among them. Wang anchored his boat near the shore, entered the wine shop, bought wine, and drank it. He enjoyed large crabs with big claws and fine-scaled fish, and the fruits included green oranges, yellow tangerines, lotus root from a pond, and chestnuts from a pine slope. He drank precious red wine from delicate porcelain cups. The innkeeper was also a wealthy person, and his daughter, who was eighteen years old, was well-educated and had an extraordinary demeanor. She saw Wang sitting there and frequently peered out from behind the curtain, sometimes showing half her face, sometimes her whole body, and repeatedly returning, unable to tear herself away.

The person also paid close attention, and they looked at each other for a long time. Soon after the wine was finished, they left the shop, boarded the boat with a sense of discontent, as if something were lost. That night, he dreamed of reaching the shop, entering through several doors, directly reaching behind the house, and finally arriving at the woman's room, which was a small pavilion. In front of the pavilion, there was a grape trellis, under which a pond was carved, circular and square, a full zhang in size, lined with decorative stone, and containing golden carp; on both sides of the pond, two weeping junipers were planted, their green shade swaying, against the wall, a screen of green cypress was formed, and below it, three peaks of stone artificial mountains were placed, towering and competing in beauty; the grass was of the sort of money-shaped embroidered cushions, remaining vibrant even in frost and dew. Between the windows, an intricately carved cage was hung, inside which a green parrot was kept, able to speak when seen by people. Under the pavilion, two small wooden cranes were hanging, holding incense sticks that were burning. On the table, an ancient bronze vase stood, with several stems of peacock feathers inserted, and beside it, brushes, inkstones, and other stationery were placed, all extremely neat. On the shelf, a jade flute lay horizontally, the one the woman blew. Below the wall, four sheets of gold flower paper were stuck, on which poems were written, the form imitating Su Dongpo's poems about the four seasons, the calligraphy and painting studying Zhao Mengfu, and it was unknown who had written it.

First poem: Spring wind blows petals to the ground, resembling a red snowfall. Willows provide dense shade, and the cuckoo bird calls. Butterflies fly from one house to another, and cherries that were planted last year are now bearing fruit this year. After playing with the swing, she brushes her hair, and the scent of sweat and perfume permeates through the green silk. The servant girl knows her inner thoughts, and she uses a silver bottle to draw water to brew new tea. Second poem: Banana leaves spread like the tail of a green phoenix. Daylily flowers contain the beak of a golden phoenix. A pair of young swallows emerge from an intricately carved beam, and several new lotus leaves float on the green water. The oppressive heat of summer makes people feel lethargic, and someone lazily picks up needlework during the afternoon, when time seems to drag on. She stands under the shade of a pomegranate tree and playfully throws a plum at a singing bird.

The third scroll reads: The sound of iron horses is loud, the wind force is intense; the dream of the mandarin ducks in the cloud window is broken, and they feel cold. The jade stove burns musk, leaving a lingering fragrance; the silk fan flutters, chasing fireflies with no fixed shadow. Who plays the flute in the cave? The Milky Way flows westward, and the moon is half slanted. To dye the delicate red fingernails, grinding the begonia flower in a golden basin at night. The fourth scroll reads: The camellia has not yet bloomed, and the plum blossoms are half open; the wind moves the curtain, and snowflakes dance. The golden plate is cold, shaping the lion; the embroidered curtain surrounds the spring, protecting the parrot. Asking someone to warm the brush to draw the eyebrows, the lip balm freezes cold, taking time to apply to the face. After finishing the makeup, leaning on the head to look in the mirror again, the phoenix hairpin is tilted, pressing on the lucky fragrance branch. The woman sees the man arrive, welcomes him, takes his hand into the room, and they engage in playful banter, spending the night together in the bedroom. They wake up at the crow of the rooster, having fallen asleep under the thatched window. Afterward, when returning home, she dreams of him every night. One night, she sees the jade flute on the stand and asks the woman to play it. The woman plays several pieces of "The Falling Plum Blossom Wind," the melody is loud and clear, resounding through the clouds. One night, the woman embroiders red silk shoes by the light of the lamp, and the man pricks the lamp flower, accidentally dropping it onto the shoe, forming an oil stain. One night, the woman gives the man a purple gold and jade ring, and the man gives her a crystal double fish fan pendant in return. After waking up, the ring is still in his hand, but the fan pendant is nowhere to be seen. The man is greatly surprised and writes a poem in the style of Yuan Zhen, thirty rhymes to record the event. The poem reads: There is a beauty in the boudoir, a celestial being who has descended. Her charm and grace are innate, and her wit and intelligence are even more remarkable.

The Jade is carved into fairy bones, and fat is refined into a beautiful form. The waist is as graceful as a willow swaying in the wind, and the character is as noble as a plum blossom in the snow. She is worthy of being placed in a golden house and suitable for ascending a seven-treasure platform. Her charming appearance should be her own, and no one can match her exquisite qualities. She rides in a fine carriage, captivating others with her true allure. Light dust arises by the Luo River, and distant paths connect to the mystical Mount Tiantai. The curtains are high and rolled up, and the door is half-open, welcoming guests. The rare herb *calamus* is hard to meet, but the young *bitter orange* flower easily contains its bud. She does not need a golden screen to enhance her beauty, nor does she require delicate hands to plant her. Her charm is like that of Jia Baoyu, and her waiting for the moon is like that of Cui Yingying. Her zither can rival the music of the Qin palace, and her qin is guessed to come from the family of Zuo Qiuming. The sound of the flute travels mysteriously, and the flickering candlelight illuminates her wandering steps. Thin windows hold the shimmering scales of fish, and a deep incense burner emits the fragrance of musk. Her eyebrows are like distant green mountains, and her hair is piled like green clouds. Her hairpins are delicately crafted from jade, and her silk robes are tightly fitted. A pair of mandarin ducks swims in the vast waters, and a phoenix dances gracefully. Her sorrows are accumulated on a silk handkerchief, and her joys are passed through a precious cup. She sympathizes with the moon goddess in her solitary sleep and often smiles at the river god. She can inspire dreams like a butterfly and attract admirers like a bee. She leans on carved railings together, and sits on embroidered cushions, close to each other. She finds sweetness in sugar cane and discovers rare treasures. Green shade shelters pairs of birds, and purple qi conceals two swords. A good night is hard to waste, and her beautiful heart refuses to be crushed. Her makeup still lingers on her arm, and her tears of farewell have already welled up on her cheeks. The dripping water does not need to be hurried, and the ringing bell should not be rushed. She has traveled through the gorge and returned to the road, seeing flowers again. Only a talented scholar can understand her, and a foolish person cannot speak to her! A humble person has sown good fortune and personally reached the land of Penglai.

Upon finishing the poem, many who enjoyed it spread and recited it widely. The following year, the young man went again to collect rent and passed by the same place. The tavern owner was very pleased to see him and invited him into the inner room. The young man did not understand the meaning of the invitation and hesitated, excusing himself and avoiding it. After settling down, the tavern owner spoke to him honestly, saying, "I have only one daughter, who has never married. Last year, when the gentleman visited here and drank wine, he happened to see her, and she could not settle her feelings for him. As a result, she contracted an illness, sleeping long and speaking to herself, as if drunk or in a trance. Despite taking medicine, it was ineffective. Last night, she suddenly said, 'Tomorrow the young master will come, so I should go to meet him.' At first, I thought it was nonsense and did not believe it. Now that the gentleman has indeed entered my place, it seems that heaven has lent its spirit and granted this opportunity. I asked the gentleman if he had married or not, and also inquired about his family background and clan. I am very pleased. The tavern owner immediately took the young man's hand and led him into the inner room, to the pavilion where the daughter lived. The doors and windows, as well as the pavilion itself, were all places he had experienced in his dream; the plants, trees, pavilions, ponds, utensils, and miscellaneous items were all things he had recognized in his dream. The girl, upon hearing the young man's arrival, dressed up and came out. Her clothes were beautiful, and her hairpins and accessories were elegant, all things he had recognized in his dream. The girl said, "Since you left last year, I have missed you deeply. Every night, I meet you in my dreams, but I don't know why." The young man replied, "My dreams are the same as yours." The girl recounted in detail the flute-playing song and the story of the embroidered shoes, and everything matched perfectly. She then took out a crystal double-fish fan pendant to show him, and the young man also took out a purple gold and green jade ring to show her. They were both greatly surprised, thinking it was a spiritual connection. Thus, they became husband and wife, flying together and returning, and they lived happily together until old age, which can be considered a miraculous encounter.

### The New Tales of the Lamp, Volume Three **The Record of the Register of Wealth and Status** In the third year of the Dacheng era (1346), a scholar from Taizhou named He Youren, pressed by poverty, could not sustain himself. He visited the Temple of the City God, passing through the eastern wing, and saw a table with a sign that read, "Register of Wealth and Status." He prayed before the deity's image, saying, "I have lived for forty-five years, with a single coat for winter and a light garment for summer, a bowl of porridge for breakfast and dinner, and no excessive or reckless actions. Yet, I am always anxious and eager, constantly worried about not having enough, feeling cold even in warm winters and hungry even in bountiful harvests. I have no place to go when I leave home and no savings to rely on when I stay. My wife and children are looked down upon, my neighbors and friends have cut ties, and I am in dire straits, facing hardships, with no one to turn to for help. I have heard that the great deity oversees the register of wealth and status, holds the power to elevate one's fortunes, and if asked, will provide answers, and if sought, will never fail to grant requests."

Therefore, I do not avoid rebuke or criticism, boldly violating authority, holding my breath in front of the court, and bowing deeply before the door. I humbly request to be informed of things that may come, to be enlightened about opportunities that have not yet arrived, to be guided out of confusion, and to be supported if I am obscure. May a dried-up fish receive a little water to revive, and a trapped bird find a branch to rest on. How can I not be grateful and deeply indebted to such great kindness! If the past is already determined, and the future is without reason, the great fate is already difficult to change, and a thin or unfortunate fate ultimately leads to no meeting or chance, I also hope for clear and evident retribution or reward, so that I may know in advance. After finishing the prayer, I crouched and hid under the tablecloth. That night, the east and west corridors, and the various departments on the left and right, were all glowing with lamps and candles, with a crowd of people. Only the office where my friend prayed was empty, with no one and no lights. Alone in the dark, almost until dawn, I suddenly heard the sound of a procession, coming from afar and gradually getting closer, almost reaching the temple gate. The officials from the various departments all hurried out to greet it. As it entered, two rows of lanterns, with a very strict ceremonial guard.

The magistrate, dressed in formal attire and carrying a simple writing brush, ascended to the main hall and took his seat. The officials gathered to pay their respects, and once their greetings were concluded, they returned to their duties. The chief of the "发迹司" also arrived from the hall, having just returned from a mission sent by the court on behalf of the magistrate. Once seated, several officials entered, wearing headbands and belts, dressed in red and green robes. They greeted the magistrate and proceeded to report on their respective tasks. One of them said, "In a certain county, a household had stored two thousand stones of rice. Recently, due to successive droughts and locust plagues, the price of rice doubled, and neighboring regions closed their grain markets, leaving fields filled with starving people. Despite this, the household opened its granaries to provide relief, selling the rice at its original price without seeking excessive profit. They also prepared thin porridge to help the poor, saving many lives. Yesterday, the local deity reported this act to our department, which in turn presented it to the magistrate. It has been reported to the heavens, and as a result, the household has been granted an extension of life by 36 years and a generous reward of a large salary."

One said, "A certain woman from a certain village showed great filial piety to her mother-in-law. Her husband was away, and her mother-in-law fell gravely ill. Neither medicine nor sorcery could cure her, so she fasted, bathed, burned incense, and prayed to heaven, vowing to take her mother-in-law's place and offering her own flesh. She was thus cured. Yesterday, a heavenly edict was issued, stating: 'This woman's filial piety has reached heaven and earth, her sincerity has moved the spirits. She is to be granted two noble sons, both of whom will serve in the court, bringing glory to her family, and she will ultimately be honored as a noblewoman in return for her virtue.' The local deity has recorded this in the book of blessings." Another said, "A certain official with the surname of a certain name holds a high rank. He receives a generous salary, yet he does not think of serving his country. Instead, he is only concerned with greed and excess. He accepted 300 pieces of silver as bribes, perverted the law in handling public affairs, and extorted 500 taels of silver, harming good people unjustly. The local deity has reported this to the heavenly court, and they are ready to punish him. However, due to his own stubborn good fortune, the punishment has been delayed for several years, allowing him to avoid the disaster of clan extermination. This morning, I was ordered to record his evil deeds in the book of evil, and I am waiting for the time to come." A third said, "A certain landowner from a certain village owns dozens of hectares of land, yet he is greedy and insatiable, striving for consolidation and annexation."

A neighbor whose field bordered the grave of the deceased took advantage of his isolated and unsupported situation, selling it at a low price and not returning the money, causing him to die with resentment. The underworld issued a decree from the office to summon his soul to hell, where it was learned that his son had been reincarnated as a cow in the neighbor's family, to repay his debt. After the officials had finished recounting their stories, the judge of the Record Office suddenly raised his eyebrows and exclaimed, "Each of you guards your duty and manages your affairs, rewarding good deeds and punishing evil deeds, which can be said to be done to the utmost. However, there is no way to change the course of events, and the time when living beings will face calamity, the national system is gradually declining, and a great calamity is about to occur. Even though the officials do their best, what can they do?" The others asked, "What do you mean by that?" He replied, "I recently came from the lord of the underworld to attend court at the imperial gate, and heard the saints discussing future events. In a few years, a great war will break out, and in the south of the great river and the north of the Yangtze River, over thirty million people will be slaughtered. At that time, unless one has accumulated good deeds and benevolence, and practiced loyalty and filial piety to the utmost, they will not be able to escape."

Are the living beings lacking in good fortune, to be in such a state of suffering? Or is it that fate is already decided, and there is no escape from it?" All present frowned and looked at each other, saying, "We do not know." So they all dispersed. Friend Ren crawled out from under the table, bowed, and explained the cause. The magistrate looked at him for a long time, then ordered a minor official to bring the records, and personally reviewed them. He said to Friend Ren, "You will have great fortune and wealth in the future, not staying long in poverty. From now on, each day will be better than the last, moving from darkness to light." Wishing to see the true details, he took a red pen, wrote sixteen large characters, and gave them to Friend Ren, saying, "When you meet the sun, you will be healthy; when you meet the moon, you will prosper; when you meet the clouds, you will decline; when you meet the lightning, you will perish." After hearing this, he put what was given to him in his bosom and bowed twice before leaving. As he walked to the temple gate, the sky had already dawned.

Hurriedly searching in his bosom, but found nothing. Returning home, he talked to his wife and children to console himself. Not long after, in the prefecture, there was a prominent family named Fu Rihui who invited him to educate their children, offering him a monthly stipend of five ingots of silver, and his family gradually became more prosperous. He stayed in their household for several years. Later, a rebellion led by Zhang in Gaoyou broke out, and the Yuan dynasty ordered Chancellor Toghon to lead troops to suppress it. Grand Tutor Darimyesh, who was well-versed in books and fond of scholars, was pleased with Youren's strategy presented at the front lines and recommended him to Toghon. Youren was appointed as a military advisor, with carriage, horses, and attendants, and suddenly became prominent. When Toghon returned from his campaign, Youren then served in the imperial court, served in the imperial library, and moved freely among the ministries, truly a person of high rank. Shortly after, he was appointed as a literary officer and an internal court censor. Among his colleagues was Yunshi Buha, who did not get along with him and was influenced by a high-ranking official, leading to his dismissal and sending to serve as a recorder in Leizhou. Youren recalled the magistrate's words about the three characters "sun, moon, cloud," all of which had come true, and deeply warned himself, not daring to do wrong.

Having taken office for two years, there were affairs to report to the provincial governor. The officials prepared the document and presented it. Friend Ren (or Ren) inscribed his title: Recorder of the Road of Leizhou, Mr. He. At the moment of writing, the wind blew the paper up, and below the character '雷,' a tail was drawn out, forming the character '电' in a striking manner. He greatly disliked it and quickly ordered it to be changed. That night he felt ill, knowing he would not recover. He arranged his family affairs and bade farewell to his wife and children before passing away. Because he carefully considered the words of the judges about the sages and future events, from the year Xinhao of the Zhi Zheng era onwards, the Zhang family raised an army in Huai East, our dynasty founded its cause in Huai West, fighting and competing, weapons clashed repeatedly, various counties along the Huai River suffered greatly, and how many tens of thousands died in battle. Therefore, it is known that throughout the world, from border to border, from the smallest personal fortunes and misfortunes to the largest national rise and fall, order and chaos, all are predetermined and cannot be changed. But the foolish and incompetent wish to apply their intelligence and strategies, only to bring themselves to ruin.

The Temple of the Wilds of Yongzhou In the wilds of Yongzhou, there is a temple, backed by mountains and facing a river, with deep and perilous rivers and marshes surrounding it. The landscape is covered with yellow grass and green vegetation, stretching as far as the eye can see. Tall trees reach up to the sky and block the sunlight, and storms and rains often arise above it. People all fear and worship it, and those who pass by must offer sacrificial animals at the temple's steps to proceed. If not so, storms and winds suddenly arrive, dark clouds and black mist obscure everything, and one cannot distinguish even at close range. People and their belongings all disappear. This has been the case for many years. During the Dade period, a scholar named Bi Yingxiang had business in Hengzhou and passed by the temple on his way. His bags and pouches were poor and lacking, and he could not set up an offering, so he paid respect and continued on his way. Not even a few miles away, a strong wind arose, blowing sand and stones, and dark clouds and black mist appeared behind him. Looking back, he saw many armored soldiers, with pursuers numbering in the thousands of chariots and tens of thousands of horses. He thought he would surely die. He could recite the *Yujuzhijing* daily, and the situation was already dangerous. He continued walking and reciting, without ceasing. In a moment, the clouds cleared and the wind stopped, and the sky and earth became clear. The soldiers and horses that had been pursuing him were no longer there. He barely survived and reached Hengzhou, passing through Zhurong Peak and visiting the Southern Mountain Temple. Recalling the previous events, he wrote a detailed account and burned it as an offering.

On that night, Ying Xiang dreamed of a swift soldier coming to pursue him, and they went together to a great palace. The guards were lined up, and the departments and offices were arranged. The swift soldier led him to stand in the great courtyard, where he looked up at the jade lattice curtain hung on the hall, with a yellow brocade canopy set inside the curtain. The lamps and candles were brilliant, the light like daytime, and the atmosphere was strict, profound, neat, and solemn, quiet without clamor. Ying Xiang held his breath waiting for orders. Soon, a clerk with red horns and a belt appeared, coming out from inside, and called out saying, "Received the order to ask who he was in dispute with." Ying Xiang prostrated and replied, "I am a poor scholar, my nature is also foolish and clumsy. I do not know that fame and profit can be sought, how could there be enough land and houses to compete for. Wearing coarse clothes and eating vegetables, just keeping to my lot. And I have never entered a public office, unable to answer the stern questions." The clerk asked, "Submitted a petition during the day, what business is it?" Ying Xiang suddenly realized and knelt and said respectfully, "Indeed, because of poverty, I left the country and sought help from others, traveling through Yongzhou, passed by a shrine. My traveling bag was empty, unable to offer sacrifices with meat and wine, offending the deity's anger, sudden storms arose, soldiers chased, in a desperate and stumbling state, almost caught, in a state of fear and urgency, nowhere to appeal, resulting in rudely intruding upon the holy, truly not by choice." The clerk went in, a short while later came out again, saying, "Received the order to be summoned for questioning." Immediately, several officials and soldiers flew into the air and left. Soon, they led an old man with a white beard, wearing a black cap and Taoist robe, kneeling at the steps.

The official announced the imperial edict and questioned him, saying, "You are a local deity, revered by many. Why do you constantly threaten people with calamity to demand worship? You have pressured this scholar, nearly causing his death. Your greed and cruelty, where can you escape punishment?" The old man bowed and replied, "I am indeed the deity of a wild temple in Yongzhou. However, the temple has been occupied by a venomous snake for many years, and I have been unable to control it, neglecting my duties for a long time. Previously, I would summon wind and rain, demanding sacrifices, all of which were done by this creature. It is not my fault." The official reprimanded him, saying, "If things are as you say, why didn't you report it earlier?" The old man replied, "This creature has existed for a long time, causing chaos and evil, unmatched by others. Local spirits and temple deities all submit to its commands; mythical dragons and poisonous snakes all follow its orders. Whenever I try to report it, it obstructs me in many ways, and I cannot reach anyone."

Yingxiang, upon hearing the words, knew it was the snake demon holding a grudge and fabricating accusations. He then fully presented the cases of harming people, causing disasters, and creating supernatural disturbances, and debated in front of the iron cage. The back-and-forth was very arduous, and the snake demon finally refused to admit defeat. The king then ordered officials to send documents to the South Yue Hengshan Prefecture and the Yongzhou City God Temple to verify the matter. Later, the replies from the South Yue Hengshan Prefecture and the Yongzhou City God Temple confirmed the facts as stated by Bi Yingxiang, and only then did the snake demon become speechless. The king, greatly angered on the throne, scolded him, saying, "Since you were an evil spirit in life, you still fabricate accusations in death. You will be taken to Fengdu and never be reborn!" Immediately, several ghost soldiers took him away to suffer his karmic retribution. The king said to Yingxiang, "I thank you for your effort; there is nothing I can give you in return." He ordered officials to bring the registry of the Bi family, and on Yingxiang's name, wrote eight characters saying, "To eliminate the evil spirit and remove harm, extending his life by one cycle of twelve years." Yingxiang bowed in thanks and returned. Upon reaching the door, he woke up, and it was just his arm resting on the table.

**Record of the Shenyang Cave** Li Sheng of Longxi, whose name was DeFeng, was twenty-five years old. Skilled in riding and archery, he was renowned for his courage and bravery, yet he did not engage in productive work and was thus looked down upon by his fellow townspeople. During the Tianli era, a friend of his father held the position of supervisor of Guizhou, so Li went to seek him out. Upon arrival, he found that the man had already died, and so Li became a wanderer, unable to return home. The prefecture was rich in mountains, and Li made hunting and shooting his daily occupation, moving in and out of these regions without ever resting, believing he had found his joy. There was a wealthy man named Qian, who was the most powerful figure in the prefecture by virtue of his wealth. He had only one daughter, who was seventeen years old, and he treasured her greatly. She had never stepped outside the gates of the house, and even relatives and neighbors rarely saw her. One night, amid wind and rain, the sky darkened and obscured. The daughter had disappeared. The doors and windows were locked as usual, and no one knew where she had gone. The matter was reported to the authorities, prayers were made to the heavens, and a search was conducted throughout the entire region, but no trace of her was found.

The old man was deeply anxious about his daughter and took an oath, saying: "Whoever can discover where my daughter is, I will give them half of my family wealth, and I will also treat my daughter as their wife." Although his desire to find her was intense, time passed slowly and nearly reached half a year, and finally, all sound and communication ceased. One day, the young man took his bow and arrows and went out of the city. He encountered a deer and chased it without letting go, crossing over hills and mountains, and deeply into valleys and gullies, but ultimately could not catch it. The sun had already turned dark, and he lost his way back, wandering at the side of ridges and slopes, not knowing where to go. Soon after, the sky darkened and the clouds thickened, and tigers roared while apes cried. Near and far, everything grew dark, as if it were after the first watch of the night. Looking far up the mountain, he saw an ancient temple and cast himself into it. Upon arriving, he found dust accumulated and walls collapsed, with animal tracks and bird prints mixed together inside. Though the young man was greatly frightened, there was nothing he could do, so he took a short rest under the eaves, intending to wait until dawn. Before he could close his eyes, he suddenly heard the sound of footsteps approaching from afar. Thinking to himself, "In such a deep mountain and quiet night, how could there be such a sound?" He suspected it was ghosts or spirits, but also feared it might be bandits. So he climbed the railings, hid under the beam, and peered to see what they were doing.

In a short while, the scholar arrived at the gate, where two red lanterns led the way. The leader wore a three-peak cap, a red headscarf, a light yellow robe, and a jade belt, sitting directly on the altar. His followers, numbering over ten, each held weapons and arranged themselves below the steps. Although the ceremonial guard was very orderly, their appearance was like that of boars and badgers. The scholar recognized them as evil spirits and took an arrow from his waist, drew it to the full, and shot it, hitting the arm of the one sitting. The leader screamed and ran away, and the group dispersed immediately, no one knowing where they went. After a while, it became silent, and the scholar pretended to sleep until dawn. Then he saw spots of fresh blood near the altar, which left through the main gate, continuing along the road without interruption, following the mountain to the south, about five miles, where he found a large cave. The blood trail entered here. The scholar went back and forth at the cave entrance, in the blink of an eye, the grass roots were soft and slippery, and he unconsciously stepped on it and fell. It was a deep pit thousands of feet deep, looking up, he could not see the sky, and he thought he was surely going to die. He faintly felt a path on the side, followed the path, turned into a dark and secluded place, within arm's reach, he could not see.

Advancing another hundred steps, the scene suddenly opened up, revealing a stone chamber inscribed as "The Cave of Shenyang." Several guardians stood there, dressed as seen in the temple the previous night. Upon seeing the man, they were startled and asked, "Who are you, and why have you come here so suddenly?" The man bowed deeply in a formal gesture and replied, "I am an ordinary person from the mortal world, long residing in the city, making a living as a doctor. Due to a lack of medicinal herbs, I entered the mountains to collect them, greedy for much and eager to gain, advancing without knowing when to stop. I did not realize I had offended the sacred spirit and am begging for mercy and forgiveness." The guardians, upon hearing this, seemed pleased and asked, "Since you are a doctor, can you treat others?" The man replied, "This is my duty." The guardians were greatly delighted, placing their hands on their foreheads and said, "Heavenly will!" The man inquired why, and they explained, "Our lord, the Duke of Shenyang, was injured by a flying arrow yesterday while out, lying ill in bed. You have graciously come here, which is a heavenly bestowal upon us." They then invited the man to sit below the door and hurriedly entered to inform those inside.

Soon after, he went out and conveyed his master's command: "I have not taken care of myself well, bringing upon myself sorrow, misfortune has affected my limbs, poison has spread to my bone marrow, misfortune cannot be avoided, and my remaining life is waiting to be exhausted. Now, fortunately, I have encountered a divine physician and received a good medicine. This means the sick person has the joy of being reborn, and the healer has the grace of saving lives. Dare not die before receiving the treatment!" The narrator then took off his outer garment and entered, passing through heavy doors and reaching a secluded room. The curtains, beds, and bedding were extremely luxurious. He saw an old monkey lying on a stone bed, with moaning sounds never stopping. Three beautiful women were serving at the side, all of extraordinary beauty. The narrator examined the monkey's pulse and touched its wound, then said deceitfully, "There is no harm. I have divine medicine, not only for treating illness but can also transcend the world. If taken, it can make one not age after birth, and can make the three lights wither. Today's meeting must also be due to fate." He then emptied his bag to take out the medicine and ordered the monkey to take it.

The evil spirits, upon hearing the doctrine of transcending the world, rejoiced at the prospect of immortality and all knelt and bowed before him, saying, "Your Excellency is truly a divine being; we are fortunate to meet you! Our lord has obtained the elixir of immortality and eternal life. Why can't we also receive a small portion of this elixir?" The protagonist then gave away all that he had brought, distributing it among them. They all eagerly competed for it, fearing they would not get a share. The elixir was the most toxic of poisons, used to temper the arrowheads and shoot fierce beasts, causing them to fall dead at the sound of the bowstring. Soon after, the evil spirits all collapsed, becoming unconscious and unaware. The protagonist, seeing the magical sword hanging on the stone wall, took it and cut them all down, killing thirty-six monkeys, both large and small. Suspecting that the three women were also demons, he wanted to eliminate them as well. They all wept and said, "We are all human, not demons. Unfortunately, we were captured by the evil monkeys, trapped in pits and traps, unable to die. Now that you can rid us of this harm, you are our savior. How dare we not obey your commands?" He asked for their names and addresses, and found that one of them was the daughter of Mr. Qian, while the other two were also from nearby towns of good families. Although the protagonist was able to eliminate the evil spirits, he had no way to escape.

In a moment of deep frustration, several elderly men suddenly appeared, unknown from where they came. They were all dressed in coarse, unadorned garments, with long beards and black mustaches. They pushed one man in white robes to the front and bowed in a row, saying, "We are the spirits of the celestial stars, long having this land. Recently, it has been taken over by a demon monkey, and we cannot resist them. We have retreated to another place, waiting for an opportunity to deal with them. Unintended that you could clear away our grievances and purge the evil and wickedness! How dare we not express our thanks!" Each of them took out gold and jewels from their sleeves and placed them in front of the person. The person said, "You all possess supernatural powers, why are you being deceived by them, why are you so weak and inferior?" The man in white robes replied, "My lifespan is only five hundred years, they have already lived eight hundred years, that is why we cannot compete. However, we live here, we do not harm people. When our deeds are complete and our conduct is fulfilled, we will be able to fly and travel through the heavens, with freedom to come and go. Unlike them, who are greedy, lewd, and violent, harming people and things. Now their wickedness has accumulated and is not stopping, the entire family will be exterminated, they have indeed incurred the wrath of heaven, it is by your hand that they will be punished."

Otherwise, how could you possibly control its wickedness? " The man replied, "The cave's name is Shenyang, but what is the meaning of it?" The other said, "The monkey belongs to the year of the monkey, so we gave it a beautiful name, which is not the old name of our land." The man said, "This place was once your home, and I am a mere mortal who mistakenly ended up here. All I ask is for you to guide me back to the way, and I do not need any thanks." The other replied, "If indeed it is so, what difficulty is there? Just close your eyes for a moment, and you will immediately achieve your wish." The man followed his words, and all he heard was the sound of a fierce wind and heavy rain. When the sound stopped, he opened his eyes and saw a large white rat in front of him, with several groups of rats resembling pigs following it. They dug a hole to the side, leading to the road. The man took his three daughters out and went straight to Mr. Qian's door and returned home. The old man was greatly surprised and delighted, and immediately accepted him as a son-in-law. The families of the other two daughters also wished to follow suit. The man married all three daughters and became wealthy and prominent. When he returned to that place and searched for the road, he found lush grass and tall trees, with near and far looking the same, and there was no trace of the old place.

**Love Minister's Biography** Lu Aiai, a renowned courtesan of Jiaxing, was unparalleled in her beauty, talent, and skill in the arts. Her intellect was keen and perceptive, and she excelled in the use of language. Because of these qualities, people admired and respected her, and she was affectionately called "Love Minister." Her beautiful and elegant writings spread widely among the populace. Men of elegance and refinement sought her company, while those who were less educated felt inadequate in comparison. Once, during the full moon day of the sixth month, the notable scholars of the prefecture gathered at the Pavilion of the Empty Sky on Swan Lake to escape the summer heat and enjoy the moon while composing poetry. Love Minister completed four poems first, and everyone else put down their brushes in admiration. Her poems read: At the eastern end of the painted pavilion, the evening coolness is welcomed, The red lotus is unlike the white lotus in fragrance. A round bright moon like water in the sky, Where is the flute playing to attract the phoenix? The moon rises from the horizon, the lake mirrors the sky, Gentle ripples reflect on the jade pagoda. Pulling the curtain to converse with Chang'e, Would Chang'e allow the song of the rainbow robe to be absent? Holding a branch of double-headed jasmine, By the time the song ends, her hair has fallen slightly.

Where the sound of jade pendants and rings is heard, a fairy passes by; I wish to ride one green phoenix together. Curved railings and straight screens, wearing a thin six-zhu robe, I am too lazy to lean on the railing. The night is deep, and the wind and dew are so cool; I am in the highest level of the jade platform. There is a man named Zhao from the same county, the sixth son, also from a distinguished family. His father has passed away, and his mother is still alive, with a fortune of ten thousand. Admiring his talent and beauty, he was invited to marry her. Lady Ai entered the household, and she was very virtuous and disciplined. She spoke only when necessary and followed proper etiquette. Zhao loved and respected her greatly. Not long after, Zhao had a relative who became the Minister of Personnel. He wrote a letter from the capital, inviting Zhao to take a position in the south. Zhao wanted to go, but he was worried about causing distress to his mother and wife. If he didn't go, he would miss out on an opportunity for fame and success. He hesitated and could not make a decision. Lady Ai said to him, "I have heard that men are born with bows made of mulberry and arrows made of thorn to shoot at all directions. Men should establish themselves and bring honor to their parents when they grow up. How can one let deep affection hinder the pursuit of fame and success? Your mother is still alive, and I can take care of her needs and provide her with good food and warmth. But she is old and often ill, and you have a long journey ahead. As the ancients said, the days spent serving the master are many, but the days spent honoring the parents are few. You should always keep this in mind. Looking at the solitary clouds over Mount Taihang and touching the setting sun over the Western Hills, you must return early." Zhao then chose a date for his journey to the capital and held a farewell banquet in the hall. After three rounds of wine, Lady Ai asked Zhao to raise a cup for his mother's birthday. She composed a poem titled "Qitianle" and sang it as a toast. The poem goes: Translation of the poem "Qitianle": Where the sound of jade pendants and rings is heard, a fairy passes by; I wish to ride one green phoenix together. Curved railings and straight screens, wearing a thin six-zhu robe, I am too lazy to lean on the railing. The night is deep, and the wind and dew are so cool; I am in the highest level of the jade platform.

Affection does not hinder one's pursuit of fame and success, yet at the farewell banquet, the *Jinlü* (Golden Threads) is sung. With white-haired, loving parents and a young, beautiful wife, who will take charge when you depart? How many years have passed? Moreover, with gloomy and sorrowful feelings, amidst wind and rain.
