The phoenix and the crane part ways, and it is unknown when we will meet again! I beg you to listen carefully once more: serve your parents diligently, do not shirk the hard work. Official documents adorned with intricate patterns and court robes woven with fine brocade await to honor your wife and mother. You must heed this: fear the setting sun on the western mountain, as it easily brings sorrow and obstacles. Hasten your return, and dance together in colorful garments. After the song, all present wept. Zhao Zi, drunk, untied the ropes and set off. Upon arriving in the capital, the Shangshu (Cabinet Minister) fell ill and resigned, leaving Zhao Zi without a place to go, prolonging his stay in the inn, unable to return home for a long time. The respected mother, due to missing her son, fell gravely ill and lay in bed. The beloved wife took care of her with great diligence, personally tasting the medicine and cooking the porridge. She sought divine protection and offered prayers to the Buddha to ward off calamities; she used gentle words and deceptions to soothe her mother's mind. After half a year of tending, the mother finally passed away. On her deathbed, she called out to the beloved wife and said, "My son, for the sake of fame and success, went far to the imperial capital, and we have not heard from him since. I, too, have fallen ill, and you have taken care of me so well! Now that my life is ending, I have nothing to repay you. I only hope that my son returns soon, and that your future children and grandchildren will all be as filial and respectful as you. Heaven, if you know, will surely not let you down!" She then passed away. The beloved wife, deeply grieved and following proper mourning rites, personally prepared the coffin. She was buried in Baiwu Village. After the burial, she mourned at the spirit table every day and night, her sorrow so great that she became emaciated and wasted away.

In the sixteenth year of the Hongwu era, Zhang Shicheng captured Pingjiang. In the seventeenth year, the Chancellor issued an order appointing Yang Wanzhe, a military commander, as the Deputy Governor of Jiang-Zhe, to resist Zhang's forces at Jiaxing. The soldiers were not disciplined and raided the residents. Zhaos' residence was taken over by Liu, a military officer, who saw Aiqing's beauty and wanted to force himself upon her. Aiqing spoke sweet words to him, bathed, entered her room, and hanged herself with a silk scarf. Liu rushed to save her, but it was too late. He then wrapped her body in a brocade cushion and buried it under a ginkgo tree in the garden. Soon after, Zhang's forces made peace. Yang, the Deputy Governor, was killed, and his troops scattered. Zhaos journeyed back by sea, landing at Taicang, and returned directly to Jiaxing. The city and its people were no longer as they once were. He returned to his old home, which was now deserted. He only saw rats scurrying on the beams, owls calling from the trees, and moss and green grass covering the steps and courtyard. He searched for his mother and wife, but could not find them. Only the main hall remained standing, and he cleaned it and rested there. The next day, he went out the east gate and came to Hongqiao. He met an old servant on the road, called out to him, and asked for details. The servant told him that his mother had left the house, and his wife had passed away. The servant then led Zhaos to the place where his mother was buried in Bai Zhu Village. Pointing to the pines and cypresses, he said, "These were all planted by Lady Liu." Pointing to the grave mound, he said, "This was all managed by Lady Liu." Grandmother Lady Liu became ill due to missing her son, and Lady Liu cared for her until she died and was buried here.

The lady, dressed in mourning attire, supported the coffin with her hands, personally carried soil to dig the grave, and wept aloud at the burial site. After the burial for three months, the Miao army entered the city, and her house was occupied. There was a man named Liu, a county magistrate, who intended to violate her with impropriety. The lady refused, and immediately hanged herself, and she was buried in the garden behind the house. S. Zhao was greatly saddened and went to the Silver Apricot Village to view her body. Her appearance was as if she were still alive, and her skin was unchanged. Zhao embraced her body and wept bitterly, fainting and then reviving. He then washed her with fragrant water, dressed her in fine clothing, bought a coffin, and buried her next to her mother's grave. He cried, saying, "Lady, you were always intelligent and wise, outstanding among your peers. Now that you are dead, how can you be mixed with ordinary people, causing the sounds of the world to cease? If there is awareness in the netherworld, I wish to see you once. Even though our paths are different, people fear and respect you, and the bond of affection is profound, and I truly do not doubt it."

After nearly ten days, on the last day of the month, S. Zi sat alone in the main hall, unable to sleep. Suddenly, he heard a faint cry in the darkness, which grew louder and closer. Sensing something unusual, he rose and addressed the sound: "If you are the spirit of Lady Liu, why hesitate to appear and reminisce with me?" Immediately, a voice replied, "I am Lady Liu. I am moved by your remembrance, even though I am in the afterlife. That is why I have come to you tonight." After speaking, a figure appeared, moving slowly toward him. When she was about five or six steps away, her form became clear—indeed, it was Lady Liu. She wore a simple, modest dress, just as she had before, with only a silk scarf around her neck. Seeing S. Zi, she bowed, then wept and sang a self-composed *Qinyuan Chun* (沁园春) poem. The lyrics read: "Three years apart, a day feels like an empty autumn. Why haven't you returned? Remember the time when my husband and I were ill, and I personally prepared medicine. I buried our ancestors, wearing mourning clothes. At night, I divined the auspiciousness of the lamp's flower; in the morning, I sought good omens in the crows' calls. Rain beat on the pear blossoms, and I shut the door during the day. Who would have known that our love would be forever separated, and our letters would become scarce? The world is filled with strife, and fate is cruel. In the golden chamber, the monkeys and cranes are startled; under the fragrant silk handkerchief, jade and flowers shatter. To learn the three virtues, one must be willing to die, to avoid being talked about by others. You think of me: perhaps only in a painting can we meet again, like Cui Hui."

Every time a song was sung, she would cry and weep several times, her sorrowful and resentful weeping almost rendering the tune unrecognizable. Zhao Ziyuan entered the room and thanked him for his filial piety towards his mother, the labor of clearing and maintaining the grave, and the virtue of sacrificing oneself, deeply moved and grateful. He then collected his tears and began to narrate himself: "I am originally a courtesan, never from a good family. Like wild chickens and wild ducks, no one can tame them at home; like willows by the roadside and flowers on the walls, everyone can pick them. I only know to stand by the door and offer smiles, do not understand to lift the tray to the level of the eyebrows. With flattering looks and clever words, I welcomed the new and sent off the old. Eating at the eastern house and sleeping at the western house, long accustomed to losing the phoenix; wife of Zhang Lang and wife of Li Lang, originally without a fixed nature. Fortunately, I was favored by a gentleman, seeking a proper household, and immediately abandoned the old stains of impurity, reformed the mistakes of the past. Managed the household chores, gathered herbs and flowers. Strictly observed the rituals of ancestor worship, diligently followed the way of respecting the mother-in-law. Treated with propriety, buried with propriety, without shame in one's heart; sang here, cried here, never looked out the window. Who would have thought that heaven was not merciful, a great calamity arrived! Fists of poison, fought in all directions; long spears and large swords, showed off their martial prowess in the three armies. Having taken over Li Song's residence, also took away Han Hong's wife. My husband is far away, I am alone. Who does not know that living by stealth is safe, enduring humiliation for a long time. But willingly accepted the jade plan, determined to sink like a pearl. Like a moth flying into the flame, like a child falling into a well, it was self-inflicted, not due to others' refusal."

This is why one should feel ashamed of being a wife or concubine who betrays her master and abandons her family, or of accepting a position and salary from someone but forgetting one's lord and betraying the country. "Zhaizi comforted her for a long time, then asked where the Lady Mother was. She replied, 'My respected mother-in-law was innocent in life and has been reborn in the human world.' Zhaizi said, 'Then why do you still fall into the realm of ghosts?' She answered, 'When I died, the underworld considered me chaste and loyal, and ordered me to be reborn as a man in the Song family of Wuxi. I was entrusted to be a man. Because of our deep emotional bond, I had to wait for you to see you once and to express my feelings, so I delayed it for many years. Now that I have seen you, I will be reborn tomorrow. If you do not abandon our old feelings, you can visit that family and see me; I will smile once as proof.' She then entered the room with Zhaizi and they enjoyed each other's company as if they had known each other all their lives. At dawn, she got up, descended the steps, and took a few steps, then turned back and wiped her tears, saying, 'Take care, Zhao Lang, this is farewell forever!' She choked up and stood still. As the dawn was gradually brightening, she suddenly disappeared and was no longer seen. Only the empty room remained, with the cold lamp half-extinguished. Zhaizi got up and packed hastily, went directly to Wuxi, and found the residence of the Song family and knocked on the door. Indeed, there was a man there who had been pregnant for twenty months. However, since being born, he has been crying non-stop. Zhaizi explained the whole story and wished to see him. Indeed, he smiled once and stopped crying, and his family then named him Luo Sheng. Zhaizi requested to be relatives, and from then on, they exchanged gifts and visits frequently, and their communication never ceased."

The Biography of Cui Cui Cui Cui, surnamed Liu, was a girl from a common family in Huai'an. Born with keen insight, she was able to understand poetry and books. Her parents did not force her to change her aspirations and allowed her to attend school. Among her classmates was a boy named Jin Ding, also from the same family and of the same age, who was also intelligent and elegant. The students would joke, saying, "Those of the same age should be husband and wife." Both of them privately considered this a promise to each other. Jin Ding wrote a poem for Cui Cui: Twelve balustrades of seven treasures, the spring breeze opens up the sunny flowers everywhere. The peach trees in the eastern garden and the willows in the western garden, why not plant them together in one place? Cui Cui responded with a poem: In my life, I often lament Zhu Yingtai, why does the sorrowful heart not open up? I wish the eastern lord would use his effort diligently, to move the flower tree to the sunny side early.

Later, when Cui Cui grew older, she no longer attended school. When she reached sixteen, her parents arranged for her marriage, and she would always weep and refuse to eat. When asked about her feelings, at first she refused to speak, but after a long time, she said, "I must marry the son of the family to the west; I have already promised him. If you do not agree, I will die. I swear I will never step into another house!" Her parents had no choice but to agree. However, Liu's family was wealthy while Jin's family was poor. His son was intelligent and handsome, but their social status was far inferior. When the matchmaker came to their house, they indeed refused due to poverty, feeling ashamed and dared not accept. The matchmaker said, "They must want to marry Jin's son, and even the parents have agreed. If they refuse due to poverty, it would be a betrayal of their sincere wishes, and they would lose this good match. Now I will tell them this: 'Our humble family has a son who roughly knows poetry and propriety. Your honorable family has proposed, how could we not comply? However, he has lived in poverty for a long time. If you demand the dowry and wedding rituals, they will ultimately be difficult to provide.' They will not care because of their love for their daughter." Their family agreed. The matchmaker then reported back, and the parents indeed said, "Marriage is based on wealth, a practice of the barbarians. We only know of choosing a husband, not considering other factors. But they have nothing while we have much; our daughter would not be able to endure it there. It would be better to let him come and live in our house." The matchmaker relayed the message again, and the family was very pleased. They then set a date for the wedding, and all the gifts and offerings, such as silk and fine fabrics, and the ceremonial gifts of sheep and geese, were all provided by the bride's family. After the wedding, they bowed to each other at the threshold, and the two met, and their joy was evident. That night, Cui Cui wrote a poem titled "Lin Jiang Xian" and gave it to her husband: ### Translation of the Poem "Lin Jiang Xian" **Lin Jiang Xian** On the riverbank, the willows sway, Beneath the moon, the waves gently play. In this moment, our hearts entwined, Our vows, like the river, unending. The night is calm, the stars above, Our love, a light, forever to prove. Through joy and sorrow, we'll stand together, Our bond, like the river, forever.

Once we studied together in the study, sharing the same writing utensils, but the old friend has become a new person. The bridal chamber is filled with the joy of spring! Sweat soaks into the butterfly powder, and the body is stained with musk dust. Indulging in rain and clouds is still unfamiliar, the brows painted like ink on the pillow are shyly frowning, do not tire of the gentle care and deep affection. Wish for you to start from here, growing closer day by day. Inviting the poet to continue the theme. The poet then continued in the same meter: Remembering the days we studied together in the study, the new person is not someone else. Coming in a small boat to visit the spring of Wu Ling: living in a fairy abode near the Purple Palace, separated from the mundane world. The vows of the sea and mountains have already been made, several times of light smiles and frowns, still speaking to people frequently. There is no other intention in mind, and after coming, who is close to me? The joy of their mutual understanding is such that even the peacock in the red sky or the mandarin ducks in the green waters cannot fully express it. Not even a year had passed when Zhang Shicheng and his brothers raised an army in Gaoyou, capturing all the counties along the Huai River, and the woman was taken by General Li, one of his generals. By the end of the Zhi Zheng era, Zhang Shicheng expanded his territory, spanning north and south of the Yangtze River, and controlling western Zhejiang. He then submitted to the Yuan dynasty, vowing to follow its calendar, and the roads became open, allowing travelers to pass without hindrance. The man thus bid farewell to his parents and sought to find his wife, vowing not to return unless he saw her. He traveled to Pingjiang and heard that General Li was now the governor of Shaoxing; upon reaching Shaoxing, he learned that General Li had been transferred to Anfeng to station troops; and upon arriving in Anfeng, he found that General Li had returned to Zhuzhou for garrison duty.

Coming and going in the Jianghuai region, he endured many dangers and difficulties, with the stars and frost changing many times, and his resources depleted, yet his heart never wavered slightly. He slept in the grass and dew, begging from people, barely managing to reach Huzhou. At that time, General Li was powerful and influential, with his authority and influence very strong. The protagonist stood by the gate, hesitating and waiting, about to step forward but unable to, wanting to speak but dared not. The gatekeeper was surprised and asked him. The protagonist said, "I am from Huai'an. Since the time of turmoil and chaos, I heard that my sister is in your noble mansion, so I traveled thousands of miles to get here, just to see her once." The gatekeeper asked, "Then what is your name? How old and what does your sister look like? I hope you can explain in detail to verify the truth." The protagonist replied, "My surname is Liu, and my name is Jinding. My sister's name is Cuicui. She can read and write. When she was lost, she was seventeen years old. Calculating by years, she is now twenty-four." The gatekeeper heard this and said, "There is indeed a Liu lady in the mansion, a woman from Huai'an, whose age matches what you said. She can read and write, is good at poetry, and is intelligent and wise. The master cherishes her deeply. You are not lying. I will inform inside. Please wait here." He then hurriedly went inside to report.

In a short while, the general emerged again and led the student into an audience. The general was seated on the dais, and the student bowed twice and stood up, explaining the matter in detail. The general, a soldier, believed him without doubt and immediately ordered a servant to inform Cui Cui: "Your brother has come from the countryside; you should meet him." Cui Cui, upon receiving the order, came out and greeted him with the etiquette of brother and sister in front of the hall. She asked about the parents and could not utter a word, but they wept together. The general said, "Since you have traveled far and endured the hardships of the journey, you must be mentally and physically exhausted. You may rest under my roof for now, and I will arrange for you later." He then brought out a new set of clothes and had him put it on, and arranged bedding and furnishings in a small study on the west side of the gate, where the student was to stay. The next day, the general told the student, "Your sister can read; do you also know books?" The student replied, "In the countryside, I have been engaged in scholarship, taking books as my foundation. I have read extensively through the classics, histories, philosophy, and literature, which I have studied habitually. What reason is there to doubt me?" The general was pleased and said, "Since my youth, I lost my education and rose to prominence amidst chaos. I am now in demand and have many followers, with guests filling my door. There is no one to entertain them, and my letters and documents pile up, with no one to respond to them. You can serve under my roof and be sufficient to act as a secretary."

The youth, being clever and intelligent, had a gentle nature and a talent that was outstanding. When he lived in the household, he disciplined himself even more, treating those above and below him with respect and gaining their favor. He wrote letters and replies that fully conveyed his thoughts. The general greatly thought he had found a capable person and treated him very well. However, the youth had originally come to seek a wife. After seeing her once before the hall, he could not see her again. The inner chambers were deep and secluded, with a clear separation between the inside and outside. He only wanted to convey his feelings, but there was no opportunity to do so. Months passed, and it was time for the autumn clothes to be given out. The west wind blew at dusk, and the white dew turned to frost. He stayed alone in an empty room, unable to sleep all night, and composed a poem: "Beautiful flowers were moved into the jade railings, and the spring scenery could no longer be seen. In moments of joy, one does not know the pain in moments of sorrow; it is easy to part but hard to meet again. When will the horse return from the frontier? This night, the phoenix dances alone in the courtyard. How deep are the misty halls and clouded windows? It is pitiful to miss the full moon." After finishing the poem, he wrote it on a small piece of paper, tore the lining of his fur coat, and sewed it in. He gave a hundred coins to a young servant and instructed him, "The weather is already cold, and my clothes are very thin. Please give this to my sister, have her wash and sew it, so I can use it to keep warm." The young servant took the message as instructed and delivered it to the sister. The sister understood his meaning, tore open the coat, and found the poem. She was deeply moved, and with tears in her eyes, she composed another poem, also sewing it inside the coat to give to the youth. The poem read:

Since the war began at the hometown frontier, old sorrows and new grievances piled upon each other! Though the heart is broken, the feelings are hard to sever; if not together in life, then together in death. She long kept her broken mirror, as did Lady Xie, and finally Cao Zhi composed a poem about a dragon. The inner feelings of Green Pearl and Jade, who today knows that this has come to me? He received a poem, knowing she had promised to die for him, no longer holding out hope, he became even more depressed, eventually falling ill. Cui Cui requested the general, and only then was she allowed to visit his bedside, but his illness had already worsened. Cui Cui supported him with her arm and helped him sit up; he turned his head and looked sideways, tears filling his eyes, and sighed deeply once, then passed away. The general pitied him and buried him at the foot of Daochang Mountain. Cui Cui returned home after the funeral, that night she fell ill, stopped taking medicine, and lay restless in her bed for nearly two months. One day, she told the general, "I left my family to follow him, and it has been eight years. Wandering in foreign lands, I have no relatives, only one brother, who is now dead. My illness is sure to take my life, I ask to be buried beside my brother, in the underworld, I might find some solace, and avoid being a lonely spirit in a foreign land." She spoke her last words and then died. The general did not go against her wish and finally buried her beside his grave, forming two mounds, east and west.

In the early years of the Hongwu reign, after the Zhang family was destroyed, Cui Cui had an old servant who made a living by trading. He passed by Huzhou and went down Daochang Mountain, where he saw a grand house with willows and locust trees, and Cui Cui and Jin Sheng were standing side by side. He called her in abruptly and inquired about the survival of her parents and old stories from her hometown. The servant asked, "How could the lady and the gentleman be here?" Cui Cui explained, "At first because of the turmoil of war, I was captured by General Li. The gentleman came to find me, and the general did not obstruct, allowing me to return. So, I have been living temporarily in this place." The servant said, "I am returning to Huai'an now. The lady can write a letter to report to her parents." Cui Cui kept him for the night and served him fragrant glutinous rice from Wu Xing, a soup made of fresh carp from the Tiao River, and wine from Wu Cheng to drink. The next morning, she wrote a letter to report to her parents, saying:

Bow to the reciprocal care of father and mother, the boundless kindness of which is hard to repay; the husband leads and the wife follows, embodying the ancient virtues of the Three Obediences. These roles are already established in human relationships, yet how many times have things been difficult! In the past, the Han dynasty was on the verge of collapse, and the atmosphere of Chu was extremely hostile. Power was misused, like holding a legendary sword upside down, and unauthorized military actions were carried out, akin to playing with a small pond's waters. Chaotic and destructive forces, like pigs and snakes, devoured each other; different types of people, like male bees and female butterflies, tried to save themselves. Unable to die honorably in chaos, they survived pragmatically in a hasty situation. Driven by war horses, they fled on battle saddles. Looking to the high heavens, they could not fly with eight wings; longing for their homeland, their spirits scattered. Good times easily pass, lamenting the loss of noble companions; a once-loving couple became enemies, fearing harm to a noble entity. Though they socialized for pleasure, ultimately, they felt gratitude and sorrow. The cuckoo's cry under the moonlight, the fleeting dreams of spring butterflies. Times change and events pass, after suffering comes sweetness. Now, Yang Su looks into a mirror and reunites with his wife, Wang Dun opens a door and releases his concubines, fulfilling a promise on Penglai Island, and meeting an old friend by the Xiao and Xiang rivers.

Grieving over one's own fate, yet not resenting the late arrival of spring. The willows on Changtai, though broken by others; the flowers in Xuandu, still unchanged from before. One might have thought the vase would sink and the hairpin would break, but who would have expected the wall to return and the pearl to be restored. It is akin to the two-life marriage of Yu Xiao Nu, hard to compare with the one-time union of Hong Fu. Heaven has granted convenience, and the matter is not by chance. Melting phoenix glue to mend the broken strings, to rekindle the tender bond; entrusting a letter to a fish's belly, carefully conveying the message. Before fulfilling the duty to serve one's parents, I first express this response. The parents, upon receiving it, were very pleased. The father immediately rented a boat and traveled with the servant from Huai to Zhejiang, directly heading to Wuxing. Upon arriving at the place where they had stayed the night before, they found it overgrown with wild grass and fox and rabbit tracks, and the buildings they had seen before were now two graves. Just as they were wondering and looking around, a wild monk passed by with his staff, and they asked him. He said, "This is the grave of Jin Sheng and Cui Niang, the general's former companions, not a place where people live." They were greatly surprised. Taking the letter to read it, they found it was just a blank sheet of paper.

At the time, General Li was executed by the state, and there was no way to inquire into the details. The father wept at the grave and said, "You tricked me with a letter, causing me to travel thousands of miles to be here, originally wishing to meet you once. Now that I am here, and you hide your tracks, conceal your shadow and form. You and I, living as father and son, what difference does death make? If you have any spirit, do not hide from me, to dispel my doubts." That night, he stayed at the grave. After the third watch, Cuicui and Jinsheng knelt before him, weeping and wailing. The father wept and comforted them, and they then recounted the beginning and end of their story: "In the past, trouble arose within the family, and military actions were taken in the counties. Unable to emulate the bravery of the Dou family's daughter, thus becoming the body of Shazhali. Enduring shame to survive, leaving home and country. Hating to match the delicate nature of a noble lady with the lowly skills of a commoner. Only knowing how to take away the laughing girl from the Shi family, how could they spare time to pity the silent woman of Xi. Calling at the nine gates but finding no way, a day feels like three autumns."

The husband did not abandon his old kindness, and the special envoy made a distant visit, pretending to be siblings, and only managed to meet once, separated by marital affection, and ultimately unable to communicate. He fell ill and died first, and I, feeling wronged, died afterward. I desired a joint burial and was fortunate enough to be buried together. Roughly like this, subtle words cannot fully express the situation. "The father said, 'I came here originally intending to take you back home to serve me. Now that you are gone, I will move your bones to the family tomb, and this journey will not be in vain.' He wept again and said, 'I was unfortunate in life, unable to see the court and family; even in death, I am without connection, unable to return to the family tomb. However, the earth's nature is still calm, and the divine order should be at peace. If we were to move again, it would only cause more trouble. Moreover, the surroundings are beautiful, with flourishing vegetation. Having already found peace here, it is not what I desire.' He then embraced his father and wept greatly. The father woke up, realizing it was a dream. The next day, he offered sacrifices at the grave and returned with the servant by boat."

To this day, those who pass by point to it as the tomb of Jin and Cui. Records of the Spiritual Gathering at the Dragon Hall In Wujiang, there is a Dragon King Hall. The hall, it is a temple, dedicated to serving incense and fire, hence it is called a hall. Some say the right cliff protrudes steeply, like a pond bank, therefore it is also called Dragon King Pond. The place is on the left of Wusong and on the right of Taihu Lake. The wind and waves are dangerous, and all waters converge. Those who pass by must pay respect at the temple before proceeding. Long known for its spiritual marvels, all recorded in Fan Shihu's compilation of *Wu County Gazetteer*. During the Yuantong era, there was a man named Zishu who became famous for his songs and poems in Wu. Because he passed by the place, he just happened to encounter a dragon hanging. It was a white dragon, with long hair hanging down, like a piece of jade. Its scales were shining brightly, like hundreds of pieces of bright mirrors. Turning and moving within dark clouds, for a long time before disappearing. Zishu thought it was the most extraordinary sight of his life, no one could match it. When the rain stopped, he climbed the temple, after thoroughly viewing everything. He then wrote a chapter of ancient style poetry under the eaves, saying: (Note: The actual content of the poem is not provided in the source text, so it is omitted here.)

The Dragon King's Hall is ruled by dragons, with its colorful structures reflecting on the riverbanks. Who dares to disobey the annual rites and offerings? Prayers for clear skies or rain are answered. Throughout my life, I have been curious and unique, traveling extensively through water and mountains in the regions of Wu and Chu. I passed under the Rainbow Bridge in a small boat and washed my feet in the Canglang River to cleanse the dust. The dragon has compassion and responds to my efforts, transforming clouds and winds into spectacular sights. Its tail winds around like a jade pillar, and its scales and armor shine like silver mirrors. Villagers bow respectfully to the dragon king, and people on boats burn incense to worship merchants. Everyone says the dragon has always been divine, bringing blessings and removing disasters, and no one dares to disrespect it. I ascended the dragon hall and conversed with the dragon, and my sincere devotion moved the dragon to grant my requests. It drew lake waves to make wine and soup and gathered river flowers as food. Large, wet characters were written on the hall, surprising passersby and angering residents. People in the world do not recognize the exiled immortal, and I laughed goodbye to the dragon, pointing out the way home.

After completing his task, he returned to the boat and lay down under the awning. Suddenly, a figure with a fish head and a ghostly body appeared from the temple, bowed before him, and said, "The Dragon King invites you." Zi Shu replied, "The Dragon King resides in the water realm, while I wander in the mortal world; as different as wind and cattle, how could we possibly meet? Even with a strict command, how could I possibly reach there?" The fish-headed figure said, "Please do not worry. Just close your eyes, and in a moment, you will arrive." Following his instructions, Zi Shu only heard the sound of wind and water. After a while, the sound gradually stopped. Opening his eyes, he saw a grand palace with guards arrayed, and the cold light was so intense that it was difficult to look at. It was truly a crystal palace. Upon hearing that Zi Shu had arrived, the Dragon King, wearing a crown, sword, and jade ornaments, came out and invited him up the steps, expressing thanks by saying, "During the day, you honored us with your excellent work. The taste is excellent, and the brushwork is wonderful. The temple gains great luster from this. That is why I have humbly invited you here, wanting to offer a return favor." They had not yet sat down when the gatekeeper announced that guests had arrived. The Dragon King hurriedly went out to greet them. Three people entered together: one wore a tall hat and large shoes, with a dignified and solemn demeanor; one wore a black hat and a green fur coat, with a graceful and carefree bearing; and the third wore a simple headband and a rustic robe. They sat in order. The Dragon King said to Zi Shu, "Do you not recognize these three guests? They are the former Prime Minister of Yue, the former Envoy of Jin, and the former Scholar of Tang, known as the Three Highs of Wu."

The king spoke to the three guests about the matter of the poem inscribed on the subject. They all passed it around to view and praised it without end. The king said, "The poet has come from afar, and the esteemed guests have arrived together, finding enjoyment and delight in this unexpected coincidence." He immediately ordered his attendants to set up a banquet in the central hall. All the items displayed and the flavors of the food and drink were not of this world. When the wine arrived, he was just about to drink when the gatekeeper rushed in and said, "The Grand Master Wu of Wu is at the door." The king hurriedly got up to welcome him. Once inside, the Prime Minister Fan still occupied the chief seat and could not be modest and yield. Grand Master Wu suddenly changed color and said to the king, "This place is the territory of Wu, you are the god of Wu, I am a loyal minister of Wu, and he is an enemy of Wu. The customs of Wu are ignorant, falsely calling the Three Highs by their titles and building pavilions to honor them. You also invited him into the room and placed him in the upper seat. Can you still forget the hatred of the past when Wu was swallowed?" He then accused the Prime Minister Fan, saying, "You have three great crimes, which people do not know, so for a thousand years, you have been able to deceive the world and steal a reputation. I will now expose you once, so that great evil will have no place to hide!" The Prime Minister remained silent and asked to hear the explanation. Grand Master Wu continued, "In the past, King Goujian of Yue was determined to avenge himself, sleeping on firewood and tasting gall, gathering strength for ten years, and training for ten years. With this, who could resist him in battle? How could he go so far as to use a woman carrying firewood, to teach lewdness, come up with this lowly plan, and not be ashamed? After Wu had already perished, he could not even remove the precious object, but instead took it along with him."

In ancient times, Jiang Ziya covered his face to execute Lady Xi Shi, and Gao Jing disobeyed orders to kill Li Hua. Comparing these two actions, which is more justifiable? This is detrimental to the state. After having conquered Wu, considering Gou Jian's character—long-necked and bird-like, capable of sharing hardships but not enjoying ease—he fled by sea, leaving a letter to Fan Li saying, "When the birds are all caught, the good bow is put away; when the rabbits are all killed, the hunting dogs are cooked. You may leave now." By his own inability to serve his master and by enticing his ministers to follow him away, he left his master isolated at the top, with the state left without leaders. How could he feel at peace with such actions? In contrast, Bao Shu recommended Guan Zhong, and Xiao He pursued Han Xin. Comparing these actions, which is more virtuous? This is disloyalty to his master. After leaving his position, he sought to rise above ordinary life. Why did he then accumulate wealth? He farmed by the sea, working tirelessly with his father, amassing a fortune, only to scatter and gather it again. What was his purpose? In contrast, Lu Zhonglian refused gold and Zhang Yu Fang practiced fasting and retreated. Comparing these actions, who is wiser and who is foolish? This is unscrupulous conduct. Bearing these three great sins, how can he be above me? The chancellor's face turned pale, and he dared not speak. After a long time, he said, "You have accused me, but I wish to hear what you are doing." Wu Jun replied, "Because of the misfortune of my family, I traversed many states, without avoiding hardships and dangers. Finally, using Wu, I avenged my father and brothers, and also avenged my father-in-law. There is more than enough filial piety."

Serving Wu until death without leaving, fulfilling one's aspirations for the ruler, even though suffering the cruel fate of execution by the sword, never uttering any words of complaint—such loyalty is more than sufficient. The ruler did not continue to employ me, and even until my death, I was able to foresee the disaster that befell Wu and consider the worries after death—such foresight is more than sufficient. If I were still alive, then the low point of seeking refuge in Kuaiji could be turned around, and the battle of Zui Li could not be won through deceit; the rulers and ministers of Yue would be too busy to have time for morning meals, and how could they achieve success in our country? I have often argued that the fall of Wu did not lie in the advancement of Xi Shi, but in my own being slandered; the rise of Yue did not lie in the use of Zong and Li, but in my own being killed. If I had not died, then Xi Shi, the woman from Zhu Luo, would have been sufficient for the enjoyment of the harem; the splendor of the palace would have been sufficient to boast of in the front hall; the Terrace of Gusu would not have been accessible to deer; the Temple of Virtue would not have quickly become overgrown with millet and sorghum! Only by harming my own integrity and injuring my own supporters did I allow my enemies to take advantage of the opportunity and my rival state to exploit the gap, which happened by chance. Was this the achievement of replacing the state or the strategy for the state? The chancellor was at a loss for words and thus vacated his position to yield to me. Wu Zixu then took the top seat, the chancellor sat in the second position, followed by Zhang Shiyu and Lu Chushi in the third and fourth positions, Zishu sat in the fifth position, and the king sat at the end. Soon, the wine began to flow and the music started. The king invited the guests to compose poems and songs for entertainment. Wu Zixu then struck his sword with his left hand and his gong with his right, singing loudly:

Sailing a grand ship, I view the ancient capital of Wu and Kuaiji. Lamenting the absence of people at the Gua Pavilion, deer roam freely in Suzhou. Recalling the sudden rise of Wu, it relied on capable people. Fighting at the Battle of Boju and entering Chu, allying at the Yellow Pool and subduing Jin. Why did the use of capable people not last? It destroyed its own Great Wall. When it rained eastward and begged for death, it began to hesitate and mourn. Floating the skin bag in the river, driving the white horse at the tide. Gazing at the old temple on Xushan, traveling far with the wind of heaven. The dragon palace is grand and majestic, the water pavilion opens for a feast. The day is auspicious and the time is good, greeting guests with their hats and ornaments. Pouring the spiced wine and drinking the fragrant wine, striking the golden bell and playing the jade chime. The Xiang goddesses and Han maidens perform dance and song, auspicious mist and fragrant smoke rise. The night is long and endless, the heart is restless and easily drunk. Stroking the busy sword and composing a song, to vent the unbalanced feelings of a thousand years. The song ends, and the Chancellor Fan holds a cup and recites a poem: Conquering Yue and subduing Wu, sailing a small boat on the Five Lakes, a proud crane, a leisurely duck. Achieving success and retiring, renouncing glory and avoiding office, the fine bow is hidden, why cast gold? Ten thousand years, the spirits come to wander, what night is this, lingering here! Blowing the flute and beating the drum, arranging the wine and food, exquisite girls and beautiful women, singing and dancing.

There is wine as abundant as a river and meat as plentiful as a hill, yet we are not joyful. How long will this state continue? Golden goblets and emerald cups, filled for you, with no set time for our next meeting. Let us enjoy this merriment. Zhang Shijun, leaning against his seat, recited: Driving a carriage to the old country, setting sail to the eastern Wu. The west wind rises in the morning, and dust covers the capital city. In life, what is precious is to achieve what one desires. Ask about the Huating crane and the Songjiang perch. Who would have thought that after a thousand years, his name would still be remembered? In the grand and impressive gathering of talented individuals, a splendid banquet with delicacies arranged like tortoise shells and fine wine poured like nectar. Graceful dancers wearing pearl shoes and wild poets striking gold cups. Who am I, and why am I also able to join in the singing and shouting? Writing poems to record these glorious events, they spread throughout the rivers and lakes. Lu Chushen then left his seat and recited: With a meager life in a small boat, the writing desk and tea stove accompany me. I keep the talkative ducks from Fuli but do not fish for the short-necked perch from Xiangjiang. The sound of the zither and flute spreads the great events, and I discard my hat and jade pendants to join the splendid banquet. Who needs Wen Qiao to illuminate the underwater world? Already, others are talking about it. Zi Shu then composed a poem and presented it at the gathering: In the deep waters of the rivers and lakes, where divine beings dwell. Pearl palaces and shell towers, no different from the world. Gold for roof tiles and jade for door hinges, screens with tortoise-shell patterns and railings with coral and pearl decorations. Auspicious clouds and mist support and carry, connecting to the three celestial lights above and the eight regions below.

Unless it is Feng Yi and Hai Ruo, who could linger here for long? In the grand hall, a banquet is held, with guests and hosts arranged in a formal setting, with many ceremonial rituals and dignitaries gathered, the servants hurriedly call out to the jade maidens to hold the ivory trays, and urge the divine maidens to adjust the green cauldrons. The long whale sings, the giant dragon dances, the turtle plays the flute, and the toad beats the drum. The pearl from the neck of a mythical horse shines on the wine vessels, and the shrimp-like curtains hang in the corridors. The eight tones play in harmony, mixed with celestial music, the sounds of the pentatonic scale are sharp and reach the clouds, the Xiang Fei sisters play the jade zither, and the Qin family princess comes to play the flute. Ma Gu breaks the phoenix meat, and Luo Fei gently strokes the phoenix feathers, Tian Wu, the purple phoenix, rushes around in chaos, and the golden branches and emerald flags sway and move. The god of Xushan Mountain, whom I admire, I once visited his shrine and tomb. The chancellor still wears ancient attire, and the magistrate still retains the manners of the Jin dynasty. Among the guests, there is Tian Sui Sheng, who eats chrysanthemum and has a clear and refined appearance, a dream that could not be seen in life, how could I expect to meet them all at once? The host's divine powers are difficult to measure, he can control the winds and clouds in an instant, he travels around the eight directions and the four seas, it is known that he is not a fish in a small pond. What luck for me to encounter him, he makes the withered and decayed flourish with a flowery wind! He treats me to the exotic delicacies of the heavenly kitchen, and drinks me the deep wine of the divine palace. The spitting pot is broken, the handle of the wine cup is broken, the drunk eyes see flowers and the ears feel hot. I do not go to the shore to collect pearls, nor do I go to the waves to catch the moon.

But the poetic lines were inscribed on silken gauze and left in the dragon palace to record the extraordinary. After the songs and praises were all finished, cups and chopsticks were intermingling in a lively scene of drinking and toasting. Only the sounds of roosters crowing in the water village at dawn and the loud temple bells being struck in the mountains could be heard. Wu Jun departed first, and the three heights followed. Wang presented a red amber tray holding the precious pearl that could illuminate the surroundings and a green jade box containing the rare item from the open sea, as gifts to Zi Shu, and ordered an envoy to return them. Upon reaching the boat, the eastern sky was clear, and the waterway was bright. He then bowed deeply before the temple hall in the middle of the stream and departed. The Legal Record of the Supreme Void Feng Da Yi, whose given name was Qi, was a madman from Wu and Chu. He relied on his talent and looked down on others, did not believe in ghosts and spirits, and would confront any strange phenomena that depended on grass and wood, that shocked the world and startled the common people. He would raise his arm to face them, and upon arrival, he would bully and humiliate them until he had destroyed their shrines or sunk their images. He went forward boldly without regard for consequences, and thus people also praised him for his courage and spirit. During the Ding Chou year of the Yuan Dynasty, he was living as an immigrant in an old nearby village near the eastern gate of Shangcai. At that time, it was after a war and destruction, and the place was desolate with no one living there, covered in yellow sand and white bones. Before he arrived, the slanting sun was setting in the west, and gloomy clouds were rising all around. There was no inn, so where could he find a place to rest? There was an ancient pine forest by the road, so he entered and leaned against a tree for a brief rest. Owls and night birds were calling from in front, and foxes and wolves were howling behind. Soon, a group of crows landed in a circle, some standing on one foot and crying, others flapping their wings and dancing, making strange and unpleasant sounds, forming a circle in formation. There were also eight or nine dead bodies lying stiffly on either side. A cold wind was blowing softly, and rain was falling suddenly. A loud thunderclap, and the dead bodies rose up in a circle, seeing Feng Da Yi under the tree, they eagerly rushed to follow him.

"Da Yi climbed the tree hastily to avoid them, as a group of corpses surrounded him below, some howling, some cursing, some sitting, some standing. Together, they loudly declared, 'We will definitely take this person tonight! Otherwise, we will be in trouble.' After a while, the clouds dispersed and the rain stopped, and the moonlight shone through. He saw a night demon come from afar, with two horns on its head and its entire body blue. It shouted loudly and took large steps, directly to the forest below, picking up the corpses with his hands and removing their heads to eat them, like eating a melon. After eating, it lay full, and the sound of its snoring shook the ground. Da Yi realized he could not stay long, taking advantage of the demon's deep sleep, he jumped down the tree and fled. Not even a hundred steps, and the night demon was already behind him. He raced for his life, nearly caught up with him. He came across an abandoned temple and rushed in to take refuge. The east and west corridors were all collapsed, only the upper part of the hall had one Buddha statue. He saw a hole in the back of the Buddha, ran out of options, and crept into the hole, hiding inside the Buddha's body. Feeling he had found a safe place, he was no longer in danger."

Suddenly, a Buddha statue laughed with its belly and said, "Those who seek it cannot attain it, but what is not sought comes to me naturally. Tonight is a good time for a snack, no need to fast!" It then rose quickly, moving with great weight, and took about ten steps before being hindered by the threshold. It suddenly fell to the ground, its wooden and earthen structure in disarray, and its internal structure was shattered. Great Yi escaped and said, "The barbarian ghost played with your master, and in turn, it brought its own disaster!" He then left the temple and walked away. Looking far into the wilderness, he saw lights flickering, and people were bowing and sitting. He was very happy and hurried to join them. Upon arrival, he found that they were all headless, and those with heads had no arms or were missing a foot. Great Yi turned and ran away. The ghosts were angry and said, "We are just enjoying ourselves here. How dare this person come to disturb us! We should seize him and make him into a feast!" They then stumbled and roared, some throwing cow dung and others grabbing human bones to throw, and those without heads carried their heads to chase after him.

A river named Yong blocked the way ahead, and a strange person crossed the turbulent waters. Various ghosts arrived at the River Yong and dared not cross. Suddenly, the strange person reached halfway and looked back, still hearing the sound of noise continuing without stopping. In a moment, the moon set, and the path became unclear. The strange person lost his footing and fell into a bottomless pit, which was identified as the Ghost Valley. The cold sand blinded his eyes, and the chilling energy penetrated to the bone. A multitude of ghosts gathered there. There were those with red hair and two horns, those with green fur and two wings, those with a bird's beak and fangs, and those with a cow's head and a beast's face. All had bodies like indigo and spewed flames from their mouths. Seeing the strange person arrive, they greeted each other saying, "The enemy has arrived!" They immediately used an iron chain to bind his neck and a leather rope to tie his waist, driving him to the seat of the Ghost King to report, saying, "This is the person in the world who disbelieved in ghosts and spirits and insulted our followers." The Ghost Jade angrily scolded him, saying, "You have five limbs and knowledge. Do you not know that the virtue of ghosts and spirits is great? Confucius is a sage, and still said to revere and keep a distance."

The *Yijing* speaks of a cart full of ghosts, and the *Xiao Ya* mentions acting like ghosts or spirits. Similarly, the *Zuo Zhuan* records events such as the dream of Duke Jing of Jin and the story of Bo You—all of these are related to such phenomena. You, as a river person, still dare to deny their existence? I have suffered your insults for a long time! Now that we have met, how can I not feel satisfied? He immediately ordered the ghosts to remove his clothes and beat him severely, causing him to bleed profusely and unable to die. The ghost king then said to him, "Do you want to turn mud into paste? Or do you want to grow to three zhang in height?" He was greatly surprised and thought that mud could not be turned into paste, so he wished to grow to three zhang in height. The ghosts then dragged him onto a stone bed and handled him like dough, massaging him repeatedly with their hands. Gradually, he grew taller, and when they lifted him up, he was indeed three zhang tall, slender and tall like a bamboo pole. The ghosts laughed and mocked him, calling him the "Long Pole Ghost."

The king then said to him, "Do you want to cook your right side into a juice? Do you want to be a foot shorter?" The strange man was troubled by his height and could not stand on his own. He wished to be a foot shorter. The ghosts then drove him to a stone bed, pressing him flat like dough, and with great force, his bones cracked loudly. They lifted him up, and indeed he was a foot shorter, round and resembling a large crab. The crowd laughed and mocked him, calling him a crab monster. The strange man stumbled on the ground, unable to bear the pain. Beside him, an old ghost clapped and laughed, saying, "You usually do not believe in ghosts, so why are you in this form today?" Then he asked the crowd, "He may be disrespectful, but he has also suffered greatly; he is pitiful, please forgive him!" The old ghost then lifted and shook the strange man with his two hands, and he was restored to his original state. The strange man begged to return, and the ghosts said, "Since you are here, you cannot return empty-handed. We each have something to give you, so that people know about us."

The old ghost said, "Then, what shall we give him?" One ghost replied, "I will give him the horns that can clear the clouds." He placed two horns on the forehead of the strange being, standing tall and facing each other. Another ghost said, "I will give him the mouth that can whistle the wind." He placed an iron beak on his lips, sharp as a bird's beak. A third ghost said, "I will give him the hair dyed with the color of the red flower." He dyed his hair with the red water, making it frizzy and pointing upwards, its color like fire. A fourth ghost said, "I will give him the eyes with the green light." He placed two green pearls in his eyes, deep and green in color. The old ghost then sent him out of the pit, saying, "Take good care of yourself. The previous small mischievous ones, please do not remember them." Although the strange being managed to get out, he now had the horns that could clear the clouds on his head, the mouth that could whistle the wind, the hair dyed with the color of the red flower, and the eyes with the green light, and he had become a strange ghost. When he returned home, his wife and children did not dare to recognize him; when he went out into the market, the crowd gathered to watch.

He was regarded as a monster, and children would scream and run away. He then shut himself in his room, refusing to eat, and died in anger and frustration. On his deathbed, he told his family, "I have been tormented by all these ghosts, and now I am dying. You can put many paper and ink in the coffin, I will sue them in heaven. Within a few days, there will be a strange event in Cai Prefecture, this will be the time when I win. You can pour wine and celebrate for me." After he finished speaking, he passed away. Three days later, during the day, there was a great storm. The fog was everywhere, and thunder and lightning shook the entire world. Roofs and tiles were flying, and large trees were uprooted. It cleared up after a night. The pit where he fell turned into a large lake, spreading over several miles, and the water was all red. Suddenly, a voice came from the coffin, saying, "The lawsuit has been won! All the ghosts have been destroyed without a trace. I will not be appointed to any position because of my integrity, but I have been appointed as the judicial officer of the Great Void Hall. The position is very important, and I will not return to the human world again." His family performed a ritual and buried him. In the air, there seemed to be a spirit.

**The Biography of the Literary Attendant** Xia Yan, courtesy name Xi Xian, was from Zhenze in Wu. He was broadly learned and well-informed, with a noble and vigorous spirit. Wearing a simple headband and a rough, unadorned robe, he wandered between the eastern and western regions of Zhejiang. He enjoyed speaking boldly and passionately about matters, doing so repeatedly without tiring, and people often admired him for his eloquence. However, his fate was quite meager, and he often found himself unable to provide for his family. Once, he sighed deeply and said, "Xia Yan, you have cultivated your character and conducted yourself with care, yet why are you unable to provide for your family?" Then he explained to himself, "Yan Hui lived in a humble lane, but was he lacking in virtue? Jia Yi was demoted to Changsha, but was his writing insufficient? Li Guang was never granted a title of nobility despite his military achievements, but was he lacking in wisdom and courage? Dongfang Shuo lived in poverty despite his talents, but was he lacking in skill? There is fate at work, which cannot be achieved by chance. I know that I must accept my fate, and dare not seek what is unreasonable!"

In the early years of the Zhi Zheng era, he died as a guest in Runzhou and was buried at the foot of Beigushan. A friend who had a close bond with him suddenly met him on the road. Seeing Yan driving a high carriage, carrying a large canopy, wearing an elaborate hat and dragging a pendant, appearing like a marquis or a high-ranking official, his attendants each holding their respective items, shouting to clear the way and following to protect him, his demeanor was radiant, no longer the same as before, he headed north and left. The friend dared not call out to him. One day, early in the morning, he met him again at the city gate. Yan quickly drew the curtain, got out of the carriage, and bowed, saying, "How is my old friend?" The friend then conversed with him, holding hands and speaking warmly, no different from their days of old. He then asked, "Not long ago, you were separated from me, yet you have now risen to a high position, established yourself in a key role. Your carriage, horses, servants, and attendants are so numerous; your clothes, hats, and accessories are so splendid. You can truly say that this is the season when a great man achieves his aspirations! I cannot help but envy you greatly!" Yan replied, "I am now employed in the underworld, holding a very important position. My old friend has asked me, how could I keep anything hidden? But on the journey, I have not had the time to fully explain. If you do not abandon me, we can meet at the Duojing Tower in Ganlu Temple tomorrow night. Perhaps we can spend some time together, briefly catching up, do you think that would be possible? I hope you will not be surprised by my presence in the underworld and will not break this sincere agreement." The friend agreed. They bade farewell and parted ways. That evening, he brought wine and went there, and Yan was already there. Seeing him arrive, Yan was very happy, greeted him, and said, "My old friend is truly a man of integrity, you can truly say that we are friends who transcend life and death!" He then said, "The pleasures of the underworld are not inferior to those of the human world. I am now the Director of Cultural Affairs, a position once held by Yan Hui and Bo Yi."

The administration of the underworld is extremely meticulous in its selection and promotion, ensuring that individuals are matched with their talents and suited for their positions. Unlike in the human world, where one can advance through bribery, family background, superficial appearances, or false reputations, the underworld ensures that each person is placed in a role that fits their abilities and character. Those who hold positions in the central government, command troops outside the capital, work in the imperial library, or govern towns and counties are all selected with great care. In the human world, it is common to see virtuous people die in obscurity with emaciated necks and yellow faces, while unvirtuous people rise to prominence. This inversion of merit and status leads to fewer good days and more bad days, precisely because of this. In the underworld, however, promotions and demotions are clearly defined, and rewards and punishments are fair. Those who betrayed their rulers, ruined their countries, and enjoyed high ranks and generous salaries in life will surely face their misfortunes here. Conversely, those who accumulated good deeds, cultivated virtue, and were in lowly positions and poor circumstances in life will surely receive their blessings here. The cycles of fate and the laws of retribution are inescapable here. After these reflections, the speaker raised his cup and drank, then raised several more. Leaning on the railing, he looked out and composed two regulated verse poems, which he recited to his friend.

Laughing, I clapped the railing and tapped the jade vase, startling the crows in the woods and scattering the birds on the shore. A river flows beneath the third watch of the moon, with green mountains on both banks, a sight that has seen six dynasties. Wealth and honor have not come to me in my old age, but do you know the truth that lies beyond the visible and invisible? If others ask about my future prospects, I would say that accumulating virtue and practicing benevolence is the smooth path. My body is drenched in dew and mist under a vast, dark night sky, and the reflection of the mountains and water blend into one. By the Iron Pot City, people enjoy the moon, and from beyond the Ghost Gate, travelers return home. Fame and success cannot buy me a thousand poems, and life and death are no different from a dream. Thankfully, I have a friend who understands this meaning, and we engage in intellectual conversation late into the night on a bamboo bed. After reciting these lines, I scratched my head and said, "The highest virtue is to establish moral character, followed by achieving honor, and then by leaving behind words. In my lifetime, I have no notable moral achievements or accomplishments to recount, but I have compiled a collection of writings, not less than several hundred volumes. I have also written over a thousand articles, all deeply researched and crafted with intention. Since the sudden changes, my family affairs have declined; there are no children to open the door, and no intellectual companions outside. Thieves have stolen, and pests and rodents have destroyed, leaving only one-tenth of what was once there—it is truly regrettable. I hope my friend will show compassion for my talent, care for our friendship, and provide material support, as Zijian did with his precious sword, and as Sima Guang did with his grain-laden boat. Use resources wisely and practice kindness without expecting returns. Inscribed on durable materials, these works will be shared with those who appreciate them, perhaps avoiding the fate of decaying like grass and trees—this would be a gift from my friend. Speaking of this, I am deeply grateful and humbled!" The friend agreed. I was overjoyed, raised a cup, and bowed in thanks, expressing my earnest gratitude.

After a while, it was just getting light in the east, and they bid farewell and left. The friend from Wu visited his family, except for the scattered and lost ones, still obtained hundreds of pages of his writings, along with the collected works *Ji Gu Lu* and *Tong Xuan Zhi* and other books. He quickly ordered craftsmen to carve the blocks and sold them at a shop to spread his teachings. Yan returned to the door to express thanks. From then on, they visited each other without interruption, and all the joys, sorrows, misfortunes, and blessings of his family were reported to him in advance. After three years, the friend fell ill, and Yan came to visit, and he said, "I serve in the Bureau of Literature, the time has been fulfilled, and I should be promoted. This position is highly valued in the afterlife, and it is very difficult to obtain. If you do not want it, I will not force you; if you want it, I will do my best to help. The reason I am so eager about this is to repay your kindness in carving the blocks. Human life will eventually end in death, even if you try to prolong it by a few years, how can you stay in this place?" The friend happily agreed, and so he disposed of his family affairs, stopped treating his illness, and died after a few days.

**A Nightly Cruise on Jianhu Lake** The reclusive scholar Cheng Lingyan did not seek fame or official rank, but had always loved the mountains and waters of Kuaiji. During the reign of the Yuan Emperor Tugh Temür (the Tianli era), he chose to settle by the shores of Jianhu Lake, reciting the lines “A thousand peaks vie in beauty, ten thousand ravines contend in flow,” and roamed continuously throughout the day. He often sailed in a small boat, using neither oars nor paddles, his sail filled by the wind and his boat gently met by the waves, allowing it to go wherever it would. Sometimes he watched fish along the water’s edge, sometimes he made a pact with seagulls at the sandbanks, sometimes he played with egrets on the reed-covered islets, and sometimes he listened to the calls of mandarin ducks by the willow-lined shore. For thirty li along the lake, all creatures—flying, running, floating, leaping—were familiar to him. He lived in harmony with them, forgetting their presence and his own, coming and going freely, no longer fearing or suspecting them. When woodcutters, farmers, fishermen’s children, and herders met him, regardless of their age, they all found him pleasing and friendly. On a late summer evening, he moored his boat below the Qianqiu Pavilion. The golden phoenix had just risen, the white dew had not yet fallen, the stars shone together, and the lake and sky merged into one color. Occasionally, the songs of fishermen and villagers, singing of lotus and water chestnuts, echoed back and forth between the islets.

令言躺在船上，仰望银河，如同一条万丈长的白练，横跨南北，细云扫过，没有一丝尘埃。他敲击船舷，吟唱宋之问的《明河》诗篇，心中飘飘然，有遗世独立、羽化登仙之感。船忽然自动前行，速度极快，风和水都迅猛无比，瞬间行了一千多里，仿佛有某种力量在牵引着它。令言感到困惑不解。不久，船到达一处，寒气扑面而来，清光夺目，宛如一片晶莹的玉田，珍贵的花草生长其中，又如一片广阔的银海，奇异的野兽和神鱼在水中游弋。乌鸦群鸣，白榆树杂乱地生长。令言猜想这不是人间，便披上衣服起身，只见一座座珠宫巍峨耸立，宫殿高耸入云。一位仙娥从宫中走出，身穿冰绡之衣，披着霜纨之帔，头戴翠凤步摇冠，脚踏琼纹九章履。两名侍女，一人手持金柄遮阳扇，一人捧着玉环如意，她星眸月貌，光彩照人。

Upon reaching the shore, the figure said to Lingyan, "Why did the recluse come so late?" Lingyan bowed respectfully and replied, "I have hidden myself in the rivers and lakes, forgetting my form among fish and birds. I have never lacked sincerity, yet I am unfamiliar with your life. How could there be a question of tardiness?" The fairy laughed and said, "How could you possibly know me? The reason I invited you here is that you have long been known for your noble virtue and great moral integrity, and there is something sincere that I wish to entrust to you to pass on to the world." She then invited Lingyan to disembark and led him inside. After walking for several dozen steps, they saw a grand hall inscribed with the words "Hall of Heavenly Chapters." Behind the hall stood a tall pavilion, inscribed with the words "Pavilion of Divine Light." Inside, there were cloud-mother screens and jade-petal mats, with crystal curtains hanging from coral hooks, making the space as bright as daylight. Two fragrant balls hung from the beams, their aroma of orchids and musk filling the air. The fairy invited Lingyan to sit across from her and said, "Do you recognize this place? It is the Milky Way, and I am the Weaving Maid, a celestial deity. From here to the earthly realm, it is already over eighty thousand miles away." Lingyan stood up and said, "I, a foolish person of the lower realms, am content to rot like grass and trees. What great fortune it is tonight to travel in the celestial realm and tread upon the divine palace. I have received boundless blessings and favors beyond my expectations."

Yet I do not know what matter the revered deity wishes to entrust, or what words the deity wishes to impart. I wish to hear in detail, to dispel my earthly concerns. The fairy maiden then lowered her head and bowed, speaking in a solemn and respectful manner: "I am the granddaughter of the Heavenly Emperor and the daughter of the Star of Spirit. Having been endowed with a pure nature from birth, I live in isolation, separated from others. How could I have expected that ignorant people on earth, who love to spread falsehoods, would falsely spread the rumor of the autumn night, pointing it out as the match for the Cowherd Star, causing the pure and clean conduct to suffer this name of defilement. The originator of this, the deceitful book *Qi Hé*; the one who spreads it further, the uncouth sayings of Chu customs; the one who elaborates and promotes this, the text *Qǐ Qiǎo* by Liu Zongyuan; the one who elaborates on the story and harmonizes it, the *Qī Xī* ode by Zhang Wénqián. Forceful rhetoric and eloquent arguments, unable to make themselves clear; vulgar words and heretical speech, reaching to what? Often appearing in written records, spread in chapters. There are verses that say: 'The beautiful woman of the Big Dipper weeps with double tears, her eyes and heart torn apart for the Cowherd.' And there are verses that say: 'Do not say that meetings in heaven are rare, for they are still better than never returning to earth!' There are verses that say: 'I do not understand the Cowherd's intentions, but I must invite the Weaver Girl to play with her golden shuttle.' And there are verses that say: 'People on earth use the needle-threading ritual, but they lack the heart to send skill.' Such examples are numerous, showing disrespect to the deities."

Without fear or restraint, if this can be endured, what cannot be endured! The official replied, "The meeting on the Qixi Bridge, the boat trip on Niu Zhi, and now hearing the words of the deities, I have discerned that these are false. Yet, are there truly such things as Chang'e's flight to the Moon Palace, the Fairy of the High Tang's meeting, the miraculous deeds of Hou Tu, and the poems about Xiang Ling's mysterious encounters? Or are they not?" The fairy, in a melancholy tone, said, "Chang'e is the Moon Goddess; Hou Tu is the Earth Deity, a revered goddess; and Yu the Great's feat of opening the Three Gorges was truly remarkable, with the shamanic deities actually assisting him. As for Xiang Ling, she is the daughter of Emperor Yao and the consort of Emperor Shun. They are all descendants of sages and virtuous people, of chaste and heroic character. How could they be like what the common people say! They are not like the Jade Emperor descending to bestow titles, Yun Ying meeting Pei Hang, Lan Xiang marrying Zhang Shuo, or Cai Luan marrying Wen Xiao, where passions are easily aroused and the facts are hard to conceal. People who write about the Moon say, 'Chang'e should regret stealing the elixir, with her heart in the blue sea and clear sky every night.' And those who write about the gorges say, 'Since the *Gao Tang Fu* was written, all the clouds and rains in Chu can be doubted.'

As for the sun and moon, two luminaries, in the primordial state of the universe and at the beginning of creation, they were already present. How can there be tales of Chang'e, the wife of Hou Yi, and stories of her stealing the elixir of immortality, and how can one recklessly compare them to the solitary slumber of the moon or the frosty nights of the sun? Clouds are the spirits of mountains and rivers, and rain is the abundant grace of heaven and earth. Why, then, should one follow Song Yu's misinterpretation and equate them with the pleasures of the bedroom, likening them to the joys of marital intimacy? Such disrespectful and sacrilegious acts are the most egregious. The Lady of Xiang, the wife of Emperor Shun, was already old by the time of his ascension to the throne. Who is Li Qunyu, and how dare he use lewd and inappropriate language in the context of the Yellow Emperor's temple, saying, "I do not know where the spirit has gone; perhaps it is among the clouds and the autumn scenery." He describes his own strange encounter, attributing it to himself, spreading falsehoods and fabrications, and trampling on moral standards! The legend of the Earthly Deity, the Tang people could not openly criticize Wu Zetian's wickedness, so they used this tale to satirize her. The common people, not understanding the deeper meaning, simply believe it to be true, even to the extent of saying, "Young Wei indulges in idle matters; do not read the *Taibai Jing* on your desk." In the desire realm, all beings have partners; those without partners are those who have no desires.

Gentlemen of learning find their own joy within the system of moral education. Why would they stoop to creating or writing in a low or vulgar manner, slandering the virtuous and wise, thereby deceiving themselves and confusing the world, and placing themselves in a state of moral wrongdoing? I am fortunate that you have come to this world. Please fully explain and clarify it, so that those above the clouds and between the stars do not long suffer from false accusations and defilement. The other person then asked, "The world is filled with lies, and the true and virtuous are often slandered. Now that I have heard the words of the divine, I know they are false. But what about the legends of Zhang Qian riding the celestial raft and Zhang Jue distinguishing the stones? Are they true, or are they mere fabrications? The fairy replied, "These matters are indeed true! The Marquis of Bohai was a high-ranking official in the celestial bureaucracy, and Yan Zhenqing was a noble officer in the heavenly court. They were temporarily demoted to the human world, but their spiritual essence remained intact, allowing them to travel to the farthest reaches of the universe and recognize strange phenomena. How could they be compared to ordinary people? You are not just a karmic connection spanning three lifetimes; how could you come to this place tonight! She then presented two pieces of auspicious brocade as a gift and said, "You may return now. Remember the matters entrusted to you, and do not forget them.

令盲 took leave and boarded the boat, but felt the high and cold wind and mist, with waves surging. Within the time of one meal, he returned to the old place, where light fog had formed and large stars were gradually setting, and the rooster had crowed three times, marking the fifth watch of the night. He took out the brocade and examined it, finding it not much different from what is woven in the world. He stored it in a chest, waiting for a learned person to identify it. Later, he tried showing it to a Western merchant, who admired and played with it for some time, then changed expression and said, "This is a heavenly treasure, not of the mortal world." 令盲 asked, "How do you know this?" The merchant replied, "I see its pattern is orderly and not chaotic, its color is pure and not mixed. When illuminated by the sun, auspicious qi rises abundantly; when covered by dust, it naturally rises and flies away. If used as a tent, mosquitoes and gnats dare not enter; if used as clothing, rain and snow cannot wet it. In severe winter, one does not need to wear warm clothing to feel warm; in hot summer, one does not need to catch the wind to feel cool. The silkworms are fed on the leaves of the *fusang* tree, and the silk is washed in the water of the Milky Way. Is it not something from the loom of the Weaving Maid? How did you obtain this?" 令盲 kept it secret and would not explain the reason. Thus, he sailed away in a light boat and did not return. Twenty years later, someone encountered him on *Yusi Peak*, where his face was rosy and moist, his eyes were clear and bright, wearing a yellow hat and a coarse robe, without a headband or sash. He bowed and asked him, then he rode on a phoenix and flew away, so fast that it could not be caught up with.

The Green-Garbed Woman In the early years of the Yuan Dynasty, Zhao Yuan, from Tianshui, lost his parents at a young age and was not yet married. During the reign of Emperor Wenzong, he traveled and studied to Qiantang, residing temporarily near West Lake on Ge Ling, where the former residence of Jia Qiuhe, a prominent official during the Southern Song Dynasty, stood. Zhao Yuan lived alone and felt bored, often lingering at the gate in the evening. One day, he saw a woman coming from the east, dressed in green and with two braids, who appeared to be about fifteen or sixteen years old. Although she was not heavily adorned, her beauty surpassed the ordinary. Zhao Yuan stared at her for a long time. The next day, when he went out, he saw her again, and this happened several times, with her arriving every evening. Zhao Yuan playfully asked her, "Where do you live, and why do you come here every evening?" The woman smiled and bowed, saying, "My home is next to yours; you just don't recognize it." Zhao Yuan tried to flirt with her, and she happily responded, so they spent the night together, becoming very close. The next morning, she left, but she returned at night. This continued for over a month, and their affection grew deep. Zhao Yuan asked her about her name and address, but she said, "You just got a beautiful wife; why do you need to know more?" When he persisted, she said, "I always wear green; just call me the Green-Garbed Woman." She never revealed her address. Zhao Yuan thought she might be a concubine from a wealthy household who had run away secretly at night, fearing that her actions would become known publicly, so she did not speak. He believed her without doubt and cherished her more deeply.

One night, Yuan was drunk and playfully pointed at her clothes, saying, "This truly can be called 'Green robes, green robes, yellow sash' from the *Book of Songs*." The woman blushed and did not come for several nights. When she returned, Yuan asked her about it, and she replied, "I originally intended to grow old together with you, but why treat me like a concubine or servant, making me feel embarrassed and uncomfortable? That is why I dared not stay by your side for several days. But now you already know, so I will no longer hide it. Please let me speak fully. I and you are old acquaintances, and if it were not for the deepest feelings stirring between us, we could not have reached this point." Yuan asked her the reason, and she said with a sad expression, "Am I not in trouble with you? I am not actually a person of this world, nor am I someone who brings misfortune to you. It is fated by destiny, and our past karma has not yet been exhausted." Yuan was greatly shocked and said, "I wish to hear the details." The woman said, "I was once a servant girl of Grand Councillor Jin Qiaohé of the Song Dynasty."

Originally, he was a son of a respectable family in Lin'an, skilled in chess from a young age. At fifteen, he served as a chess boy, and whenever Autumn Ravine returned from court, he would sit and enjoy himself in the Half-Idle Hall, surely calling the boy to play chess with him, showing great favor. At that time, the master was a servant in his household, whose duty was to prepare tea. Whenever he served tea, he could enter the inner hall. The master was young and had a beautiful appearance; the boy saw him and admired him, once secretly giving the master a embroidered silk money box. The master also gave him a tortoise shell and ointment box as a gift. Although they both had feelings for each other, the strict rules of the inner and outer quarters prevented them from being together. Later, his peers discovered this and reported it to Autumn Ravine, and they were both ordered to commit suicide at the Broken Bridge in West Lake.
